user_input: How does the periodic transmission of Pdelay_Req by a Time Master, as specified by GlobalTimeTxPdelayReqPeriod, interact with the propagation delay measurement requirements for Time Slaves when GlobalTimeTxPdelayReqPeriod is greater than 0?
reference: When GlobalTimeTxPdelayReqPeriod is greater than 0, both the Time Master and Time Slaves are required to periodically transmit Pdelay_Req messages for latency calculation. The Time Master transmits Pdelay_Req messages at the interval specified by GlobalTimeTxPdelayReqPeriod, and the Time Slaves must measure the propagation delay using these Pdelay_Req messages along with the corresponding Pdelay_Resp and Pdelay_Resp_Follow_Up messages. This ensures that the propagation delay is measured cyclically and used to adjust the Time Bases, particularly for the Time Domain with the lowest Time Domain ID.
reference_contexts:
Time Master and Time Slave shall observe the Pdelay timeout
as given by PdelayRespAndRespFollowUpTimeout , if a Pdelay_Req has been
transmitted (waiting for Pdelay_Resp) or if a Pdelay_Resp has been received (wait-
ing for Pdelay_Resp_Follow_Up). A value of 0 deactivates this timeout observation.
⌋(RS_TS_20048, RS_TS_20051)
[PRS_TS_00210]⌈ If a reception timeout occurs (refer to [PRS_TS_00164]), any re-
ceived Pdelay_Resp resp Pdelay_Resp_Follow_Up shall be ignored, until a new
Pdelay_Req has been sent.⌋(RS_TS_20048, RS_TS_20051)
[PRS_TS_00140]⌈ If GlobalTimeTxPdelayReqPeriod equals 0, Time Master
and Time Slave shall not measure the propagation delay . The Time Slave shall use
a static value GlobalTimePropagationDelay as propagation delay instead. ⌋
(RS_TS_20066)
Note: Since GlobalTimeTxPdelayReqPeriod is ECU specific, neither a Time Mas-
ter nor all Time Slaves have to measure the propagation delay . Global Time Synchro-
nization in AUTOSAR does yet not define dynamic reconfiguration or backup strategies
that will reassign the role as Time Master, therefore propagation delay measurements
make currently no sense for a Time Master (although a Time Master shall be able to
handle Pdelay_Req initiated by a Time Slave).
[PRS_TS_00141]⌈ If GlobalTimeTxPdelayReqPeriod is greater than 0, Time
Master and Time Slave shall cyclically measure the propagation delay us-
ing Pdelay_Req, Pdelay_Resp, Pdelay_Resp_Follow_Up as defined in [1,
IEEE802.1 AS] chapter 11.1.2 "Propagation delay measurement". ⌋(RS_TS_20048,
RS_TS_20066)
[PRS_TS_00149]⌈ If GlobalTimeTxPdelayReqPeriod is greater than 0, Time
Master and Time Slave shall cyclically measure the propagation delay only on that
Time Domain with the lowest Time Domain ID and shall use this value to adjust all
corresponding Time Bases.⌋(RS_TS_20048, RS_TS_20066)
Note: There is no need to measure the propagation delay for all Time Domains, be-
cause the same value is expected. This requirement ensures also the usage of Time
Domain 0 for Pdelay, to be compatible to [1, IEEE 802.1 AS].
[PRS_TS_00142]⌈ If GlobalTimeTxPdelayReqPeriod is greater than 0,Glob-
alTimePropagationDelay shall be used as default value for the propagation
delay , until first valid propagation delay has been measured. ⌋(RS_TS_20048,
RS_TS_20066)

Acting as Time Master
A Time Master is an entity which is the master for a certain Time Base and which
propagates this Time Base to a set of Time Slaves within a certain segment of a com-
munication network, being a source for this Time Base.
If a Time Master is also the owner of the Time Base then he is the Global Time master.
A Time Gateway typically consists of one Time Slave and one or more Time Masters.
When mapping time entities to real ECUs, an ECU could be Time Master (or even
Global Time Master) for one Time Base and Time Slave for another Time Base.
5.6.2.1   If GlobalTimeTxPdelayReqPeriod : is greater than 0,
Time Master and Time Slave shall periodically transmit Pdelay_Req for latency
calculation with the cycle GlobalTimeTxPdelayReqPeriod as defined in [1,
IEEE 802.1 AS] chapter 11.1.2 "Propagation delay measurement". ⌋(RS_TS_20048,
RS_TS_20066)
Note: GlobalTimePdelayRespEnable allows disabling of Pdelay_Resp and Pde-
lay_Resp_Follow_Up, if no Pdelay_Req is expected to be received, i.e. for the
Time Master, if all Time Slaves have set GlobalTimeTxPdelayReqPeriod to 0 or
for any Time Slave if the Time Master has set GlobalTimeTxPdelayReqPeriod to
0.

user_input: How do the safety extensions described in the 'Overview of Functional Safety Measures in AUTOSAR' document interact with the requirement RS_METH_00081, which mandates that the methodology explain the typical usage of these safety extensions?
reference: The safety extensions in the 'Overview of Functional Safety Measures in AUTOSAR' document provide a standardized way to enhance AUTOSAR models with functional safety information. These extensions enable the description and exchange of safety concepts, traceability between safety elements and requirements, and declaration of safety mechanisms. Requirement RS_METH_00081 mandates that the methodology explain the typical usage of these safety extensions, ensuring that users understand how to apply and document these safety measures within the AUTOSAR framework.
reference_contexts:
Overview of Functional Safety Measures in AUTOSAR 
AUTOSAR CP Release 4.4.0  
60 of 96 Document ID 664: AUTOSAR_TR_OverviewOfFunctionalSafetyMeasuresInAUTOSAR 
 
 3.5 Safety related Extensions of Methodology and Templates 
The document “Safety Extensions” provides requirements upon extensions in 
AUTOSAR Methodology and Templates to realize and document functional safety of 
AUTOSAR systems. It specifies,  how the AUTOSAR meta -model is to be used to 
enhance AUTOSAR models by information for functional safety. With the safety 
extensions, it is possible to: 
 Describe and exchange the part of a (technical) safety concept of a system which 
is relevant for the realization of that system using the AUTOSAR architecture in a 
standardized form by means of the AUTOSAR templates.  
 Provide traceability between safety-related elements of the AUTOSAR model and 
the safety requirements as part of the AUTOSAR templates. 
 Declare the safety mechanisms/safety measures44 that are applied for an 
AUTOSAR system as part of the AUTOSAR templates. 
 Demonstrate the traceability between safety mechanisms/safety measures and 
safety requirements as part of the AUTOSAR templates. 
All the safety measures and mechanisms described in “Overview of Functional Safety 
Measures in AUTOSAR” can be modeled and traced using the Safety Extensions as 
explained above.

user_input: How does the definition of the Application ID and Context ID in the trace message structure (from Context 1) influence the implementation of the SetLogLevel service (from Context 2) in terms of controlling the log levels for specific applications and contexts?
reference: The Application ID and Context ID in the trace message structure uniquely identify the source of log and trace messages. In the SetLogLevel service, these identifiers are used as parameters to control the log levels for specific applications and contexts. If the Application ID is provided and the Context ID is NULL, the log level is set for all Context IDs associated with that Application ID. If both Application ID and Context ID are provided, the log level is set specifically for that combination. This allows fine-grained control over log levels for different parts of the software.
reference_contexts:
Trace Message A Trace messages contains information, which has passed via the VFB. 
ECU ID 
 
The ECU ID is the name of an ECU, composed by four 8-bit ASCII 
characters (e.g. ABS0 or COMB).  
FIBEX The Field Bus Exchange Format is a versatile XML bases description 
format. 
Session A session is the logical entity of the source of log or trace messages. If a 
SW-C / application is inst antiated several times , a session for each 
instance with a globally unique session ID is used. 
Session ID The Session ID is the identification number of a log or trace session 
Application ID 
 
Application ID is an abbreviation of a SW-C / application.  
It identifies the SW-C / application the log and trace message originates. 
Context ID 
 
Context ID is a user defined ID to group Log and Trace Messages 
generated by a SW-C / application. The following rules apply:  
 Each Application ID can own several Context IDs.  
 Context IDs are grouped by Application IDs.  
 Context IDs shall be unique within an Application ID. 
 The source of a log and trace message is identified using 
the tuple “Application ID” and “Context ID”.  
 
Four 8-bit ASCII characters compose the Context ID. 
Message ID 
 
Messaged ID is the ID to characterize the information, which is transported 
by the message itself. A Message ID identifies a log or trace message 
uniquely. It can be used for identifying the source (in source code) of a 
message and it can be used for characterizing the payload of a message. 
A Message ID is statically fixed at development or configuration time. 
Log level A log level defines a classification for the severity grade of a Log Message.  
Trace status The trace status provides information if a trace message should be send. 
Log Channel A physical Communications Bus which is used to transmit Dlt messages.  
External client 
 
The external client is a tool to control, monitor and store the log/trace 
information provided by the ECUs using the Dlt module.

Log and Trace Protocol Specification 
AUTOSAR FO Release 1.5.0

user_input: How does the 8-bit CRC used in E2E Profile 2, as described in Context 1, influence the detection of masquerading faults compared to the 32-bit CRC used in E2E Profile 4, as described in Context 2?
reference: The 8-bit CRC used in E2E Profile 2 has a limited detection capability for masquerading faults, which means such faults may not be detected in a specific cycle. Instead, they are detected over successive communication cycles due to the use of a pre-defined DataIDList. In contrast, the 32-bit CRC used in E2E Profile 4 provides a higher detection rate and greater Hamming Distance, allowing for more immediate and reliable detection of masquerading faults.
reference_contexts:
CRC Corruption, Asymmetric information 4
Table 6.14: Detectable communication faults using Profile 2
[PRS_E2E_00117]⌈E2E Profile 2 shall use the Crc_CalculateCRC8H2F() function of
the SWS CRC Supervision for calculating CRC checksums. ⌋(RS_E2E_08528)
[PRS_E2E_00118]⌈E2E Profile 2 shall use 0xFF as the start value CRC_StartValue8
for CRC calculation.⌋(RS_E2E_08528)
[PRS_E2E_00119]⌈In E2E Profile 2, the specific Data ID used to calculate a specific
CRC shall be of length 8 bit. ⌋(RS_E2E_08528)
[PRS_E2E_00120]⌈In E2E Profile 2, the specific Data ID used for CRC calculation
shall be selected from a pre-defined DataIDList[16] using the value of the Counter as
an index.⌋(RS_E2E_08528)
Each data, which is protected by a CRC owns a dedicated DataIDList which is de-
posited on the sender site and all the receiver sites.
The pre-defined DataIDList[16] is generated offline. In general, there are several fac-
tors influencing the contents of DataIDList, e.g:
1. length of the protected data
2. number of protected data elements
3. number of cycles within a masquerading fault has to be detected
4. number of senders and receivers
5. characteristics of the CRC polynomial.
Due to the limited length of the 8bit polynomial, a masquerading fault cannot be de-
tected in a specific cycle when evaluating a received CRC value. Due to the adequate
Data IDs in the DataIDList, a masquerading fault can be detected in one of the succes-
sive communication cycles.
Due to the underlying rules for the DataIDList, the system design of the application has
to take into account that a masquerading fault is detected not until evaluating a certain
number of communication cycles.
E2E Protocol Specification
AUTOSAR FO Release 1.5.0
expects only a specific data element, which is checked by E2E Supervision using Data
ID.
In case of usage of E2E Supervision for protecting I-PDUs (i.e. invocation from COM),
the receiver COM expects at a reception only a specific I-PDU, which is checked by
E2E Supervision using Data ID.
 6.5.4 Length
The Length field is introduced to support variable-size length - the Data [] array storing
the serialized data can potentially have a different length in each cycle. The Length
includes user data + E2E Header (CRC + Counter + Length + DataID).
 6.5.5 CRC
E2E Profile 4 uses a 32-bit CRC, to ensure a high detection rate and high Hamming
Distance.
[PRS_E2E_00329] ⌈E2E Profile 4 shall use the Crc_CalculateCRC32P4 () func-
tion of the SWS CRC Supervision for calculating the CRC. ⌋(RS_E2E_08528,
RS_E2E_08539)
Note: The CRC used by E2E Profile 4 is different from the CRCs used by FlexRay ,
CAN and TCP/IP . It is also provided by different software modules (FlexRay , CAN and
TCP/IP stack CRCs/checksums are provided by hardware support in Communication
Controllers or by communication stack software, but not by CRC Supervision).
[PRS_E2E_00330]⌈ In E2E Profile 4, the CRC shall be calculated over the entire E2E
header (excluding the CRC bytes) and over the user data. ⌋(RS_E2E_08536)

user_input: How does the error handling mechanism described in the AUTOSAR standard for NVRAM manager functions (such as NvM_ReadAll) impact the initialization process of RAM blocks from NVRAM, particularly when using the NvM_ReadAll function?
reference: The error handling mechanism for NVRAM manager functions, including NvM_ReadAll, ensures that any errors detected during the initialization process are managed appropriately. If an error occurs during NvM_ReadAll, the NVRAM manager will attempt recovery mechanisms. If these fail, the error NVM_E_REQ_FAILED is reported to the DEM, and the error status can be polled by the user using NvM_GetErrorStatus or reported via configurable callbacks. This allows the system to handle and report errors during the initialization of RAM blocks from NVRAM, ensuring that issues are identified and addressed.
reference_contexts:
Description of the AUTOSAR standard errors 
AUTOSAR CP Release 4.4.0   
79 of 79 Document ID 377: AUTOSAR_EXP_ErrorDescription 
[SWS_NvM_00359] [SWS_NvM_00213] [SWS_NvM_00271] The 
NVRAM manager is notified of an error detected by the subsequent 
layers during the process of a NVM function (NvM_ReadAll, 
NvM_InvalidateNvBlock, NvM_WriteAll, NvM_ReadBlock, 
NvM_WriteBlock, NvM_EraseNvBlock) 
 Reaction N/A 
 Report  [SWS_NvM_00213] [ SWS_NvM_00296] [ SWS_NvM_00279] 
[SWS_NvM_00288] If the recovery mechanisms fail (see below), the 
error NVM_E_REQ_FAILED is reported to the DEM. 
Depending on the configuration : 
 [SWS_NvM_00451] [ SWS_NvM_00295] [ SWS_NvM_00204] The 
error shall be polled by the user with the function 
NvM_GetErrorStatus (job result set to NVM_REQ_NOT_OK). 
 [SWS_NvM_00113] [SWS_NvM_00260] The error shall be reported 
to the user via the configurable callbacks 
SingleBlockCallbackFunction or MultiBlockCallbackFunction.

Initialization of RAM Blocks from NVRAM 
There are different strategies in AUTOSAR to restore RAM blocks to their previous 
values i.e. the values held before going into last shutdown. 
 
Individual blocks can be explicitly read one -by-one using 
NvM_ReadBlock/NvM_ReadPRAMBlock from the Initialization runnables called using 
InitEvent during Rte_Init(). 
 
A more optimized approach is to read all such bloc ks where data  persistence is 
required using a single NvM request NvM_ReadAll. All such NvM blocks are 
configured to be applicable for NvM_ReadAll. Any block to be read during 
NvM_ReadAll has to either have explicit synchronization or have a permanent RAM 
block. Figure 4 shows the process of restoration of RAM block using NvM_ReadAll. 
Further information about the AUTOSAR startup process is mentioned in [4].

user_input: How does the ClientServerToSignalMapping in the SystemTemplate package relate to the TriggerToSignalMapping in terms of their respective mappings to SystemSignals?
reference: The ClientServerToSignalMapping and TriggerToSignalMapping both map elements to SystemSignals, but they serve different purposes. The ClientServerToSignalMapping maps a ClientServerOperation to call and return SystemSignals, where the callSignal is used for IN and INOUT arguments, and the returnSignal is used for OUT and INOUT arguments. In contrast, the TriggerToSignalMapping maps a Trigger to a single SystemSignal, which is used to transport the Trigger over the network, and the Trigger does not carry any additional information beyond its existence.

user_input: How does the initial configuration of the E2E Profile 4, specifically the MaxDeltaCounter value, influence the tolerance for lost messages as described by the maxDeltaCounterInit parameter in the legacy profiles?
reference: The initial configuration of E2E Profile 4 sets the MaxDeltaCounter to 1, which aligns with the maxDeltaCounterInit parameter in the legacy profiles. This value determines the maximum allowed gap between two consecutive counter values, thereby influencing the system's tolerance for lost messages. A MaxDeltaCounter of 1 means the system can tolerate a single lost message before considering the communication abnormal.

user_input: How does the use of cross-core ExclusiveAreas for accessing shared resources, as described in the BSW module implementation, interact with the constraints for access serialization across cores to ensure concurrency safety in a multi-core environment?
reference: Cross-core ExclusiveAreas, such as protected spinlocks, are used to ensure controlled access to shared resources in a multi-core environment. These ExclusiveAreas provide a mechanism for access serialization across cores, preventing concurrent access to the same resource. By implementing these cross-core mechanisms, the BSW modules can achieve concurrency safety, aligning with the requirement that exclusive areas must serialize access not only within a single core but also across multiple cores.
reference_contexts:
Access to all shared resources, e.g. buffers, is protected by ExclusiveAreas.  
 Call trees can be made multi-core safe, if either called entities are safe, or calls 
are protected by ExclusiveAreas (if lock times stay within a specified limit). 
 
BswCalledEntities that are available to CDDs can also be called directly by the CDD. 
The same rules apply as in R4.0. 
The SchM must support cross core ExclusiveAreas, implemented by protected 
Spinlocks. A protected spinlock is an exclusive area that has "OS_SPINLOCK" as its 
value of "RteExclusiveAreaImplMechanism". This kind of exclusive areas is available 
for controlled access by BSW modules only. Protected spinlocks are handled by the 
Basic Software Scheduler. 
 
2.2.5 Accessing Hardware/Drivers  
BswModuleEntities of the MCAL (drivers) shall be accessed in the following way: 
 Access by the BSW functional cluster within the BSW partition where the caller is 
located. So for example the FLS driver belongs to the BSW functional cluster 
“Memory”. In case of NVM access, the NVM module might be provided on all 
cores as a master/satellite implementation. The master uses the FLS driver on a 
single core only. So the FLS driver is available on exactly that core. 
 Any BSW required by the application shall be accessed in the BSW partition 
where the caller is located. For example I/O drivers such as DIO, ADC and PWM 
can be used by any core / partition. These are either realized as master/satellite 
implementation or as a redundant implementation per core basing on atomic 
access to the hardware. 
The detailed realization of the MCAL multi-core approach is described in chapter 2.5 
MCAL Distribution. 
 
2.2.6 Concurrency safe implementation of modules 
Concurrency safety of BSW modules respectively the functions implemented by 
these modules may be achieved by different mechanisms. 
Generally, the following levels of reentrancy can be distinguished according to 
(TPS_BSWMDT_04103). The concrete level of a BswModuleEntity is defined in the 
optional attribute “reentrancyLevel”.  
 Multi-core reentrant: Unlimited concurrent execution of an interface is 
possible, including preemption and parallel execution on multi-core systems.

Constraints 
To realize the concept further constraints are defined to prevent inefficient and multi-
core blocking implementations. In this sense it is especially important to consider that 
it is not sufficient anymore to implement exclusive areas on a single core but to 
additionally ensure an access serialization in case resources need to be shared 
across several partitions distributed to several cores. 
 
1.  Access Serialization on a single core: For single-core systems concurrency 
problems are well understood and mitigated by exclusive areas, which limit 
concurrent access to one process at a time. This is typically done by locking 
interrupts, employing OS resources or creating a non-pre-emptive scheduling. 
This effectively means access-serialization of the different processes. 
2.  Access Serialization across cores: Since exclusive areas only have a core-
wide scope, they are not sufficient to prevent concurrent access in multi-core 
environments. But as soon as it is required to access the same resource (e.g. 
by access to the service API’s, processing of the ISRs and main-functions) it is 
required to introduce cross-core means. Besides the usage of atomic 
resources, the worst – because blocking – one would be the introduction of a

user_input: Given the requirement that AUTOSAR shall support multi-core MCUs with only one common binary managing one or more of the MCU cores, what are the implications for the distribution of MCAL modules across multiple cores, and how does this affect the need for asynchronously behaving interfaces?
reference: The requirement for AUTOSAR to support multi-core MCUs with a single common binary implies that MCAL modules must be distributed across multiple cores based on their specific needs and the functional clusters they serve. This distribution necessitates the introduction of asynchronously behaving interfaces to ensure non-blocking parallel execution, enabling efficient and concurrent access to hardware features from different cores.

2.5 MCAL Distribution 
 2.5.1 Introduction 
Because it is required to provide access to hardware features from several cores and 
partitions the MCAL functionality needs to be provided to exactly that core it is 
required and where it is useful to provide the functionality. So consequently the 
distribution of MCAL modules is not identically done for all MCAL modules but needs 
to follow the needs of the functional clusters described in the chapters before. The 
following chapters shall guide through the classification of the required multi-core 
capabilities, introduce an according multi-core type which is assigned to the 
individual modules. Furthermore some basic design patterns shall be shown to allow 
the implementation of the required functionality. 
It shall be noted that the introduction of the multi-core MCAL requires the introduction 
of asynchronously behaving interfaces to enable non-blocking parallel execution on 
multiple cores. These are introduced to the individual SWS of the affected AUTOSAR 
modules and not mentioned furthermore in the chapter below.

user_input: Given that a union can carry data of different types and requires metadata to identify the applicable data type, how does the length field and type field in the serialized data stream ensure proper data alignment and identification when transmitting a union containing a uint8 and a uint16?
reference: The length field and type field in the serialized data stream ensure proper data alignment and identification by specifying the size of the data and padding, and by indicating the specific data type of the union. The length field defines the size of the data and padding in bytes, ensuring that the data is aligned to the required boundary. The type field specifies the data type of the data, allowing the receiver to correctly interpret the data. For example, if the union supports a uint8 and a uint16, both are padded to the 32-bit boundary, and the type field indicates whether the data is a uint8 or a uint16, ensuring that the receiver can access the data properly.
reference_contexts:
4.1.4.9 Union / Variant
There are use cases for defining data as unions on the network where the payload can
be of different data types.
A union (also called variant) is such a parameter that can contain different types of
data. For example, if one defines a union of type uint8 and type uint16, the union shall
carry data which are a uint8 or a uint16.
Which data type will be transmitted in the payload can only be decided during execu-
tion. In this case, however, it is necessary to not only send the data itself but add an
information about the applicable data type as a form of "meta-data" to the transmission.
By the means of the attached meta-data the sender can identify the applicable data
type of the union and the receiver can accordingly access the data properly .
[PRS_SOMEIP_00118]⌈ A union shall be used to transport data with alternative data
types over the network. ⌋(RS_SOMEIP_00034)

user_input: How does the integration of Non-AUTOSAR-ECUs, as described in the introduction, impact the problems of mapping ports and interfaces to predefined communication messages in the interaction with Non-AUTOSAR-ECUs?
reference: The integration of Non-AUTOSAR-ECUs, which includes ECUs not developed according to AUTOSAR mechanisms, existing ECUs in a system, and intelligent sensors/actuators without AUTOSAR VFB/RTE, introduces the problem of mapping ports and interfaces to predefined communication messages. This mapping is necessary to route communication through gateways, but it results in unconnected ports of AUTOSAR SW-Components and hidden communication load, complicating the interaction between AUTOSAR and Non-AUTOSAR components.
reference_contexts:
Virtual Functional Bus 
AUTOSAR CP Release 4.4.0   
 
101 of 104 Document ID 056: AUTOSAR_EXP_VFB 
12  Interaction with Non-AUTOSAR-ECUs 
 12.1 Introduction 
This section describes the interaction with Non -AUTOSAR-ECUs on VFB level. This 
kind of interaction is e.g. necessary to provide a migration path. 
Non-AUTOSAR-ECUs are: 
ECUs that have not been developed according to AUTOSAR mechanisms. This is useful 
for e.g.: 
o Integration of an AUTOSAR ECU into an already existing system of ECUs 
o Connect system of AUTOSAR ECUs to already existing system of ECUs 
o Re-use already existing ECU in system of AUTOSAR ECUs 
ECUs that have been developed according to AUTOSAR mechanisms  once, but stay 
unchanged now. This is useful for e.g.: 
o Reuse strategies (taking over of complete unchangeable AUTOSAR (!!!) 
ECUs) 
Intelligent ('Smart') Sensors/Actuators with an ECU which do not implement the 
AUTOSAR VFB / AUTOSAR RTE. This is useful for e.g.: 
o Using Commercial of the shelf LIN nodes. 
Interaction of A UTOSAR SW-C with non AUTOSAR software within one ECU is not 
analyzed in this document.

12.2 Problems of interaction 
The following problems will arise from the interaction with Non-AUTOSAR-ECUs: 
 
 Interaction with interfaces of applications on Non-AUTOSAR-ECUs: 
 Ports/Interfaces have to be mapped to pre -defined communication messages 
(possible to be routed through gateway) 
 Non-AUTOSAR-SW-Components are currently not modeled at VFB level 
o  Unconnected ports of AUTOSAR-SW-Components 
o  Hidden communication load 
 Client-Server not supported in old systems. 
 
 Interaction/support of services implemented on Non-AUTOSAR ECUs 
 Old services/protocols have to be supported in parallel, to enable interoperab ility, e.g. 
Network Management.  
 Additional services supported by communication system (e.g. bus sleep/bus 
wake-up). 
 LIN nodes inherently are not affected because it is using the master slave paradigm  
o  services/protocols have to be managed and implemented in any case 
by master node (in this case AUTOSAR ECU) 
o  Required configuration data available in node capability file (NCF) 
 
Problem of support of enhanced services/protocols (e.g. Network Management, 
Diagnosis (connection to AUTOSAR SW-C), Transport Protocol Layer, ...)

user_input: How does the use of a 4-bit counter in End-2-End Profile 1, as described in the protection mechanisms, contribute to the detection of faults such as repetition, deletion, and incorrect sequence, and what are the implications of this mechanism for the overall configuration of End-2-End profiles in AUTOSAR?
reference: The 4-bit counter in End-2-End Profile 1 is incremented with each send request and is explicitly sent. The receiver checks the counter value to determine if it has increased, which helps detect faults such as repetition, deletion, and incorrect sequence. This mechanism ensures that messages are received in the correct order and that no messages are missed or duplicated. The use of this counter, along with other mechanisms like the CRC checksum and Data ID, contributes to the robustness and reliability of the End-2-End profile, aligning with the AUTOSAR standard's requirement for standardized and configurable protection mechanisms.
reference_contexts:
Overview of Functional Safety Measures in AUTOSAR 
AUTOSAR CP Release 4.4.0  
40 of 96 Document ID 664: AUTOSAR_TR_OverviewOfFunctionalSafetyMeasuresInAUTOSAR 
 2.4.2.1 End-2-End Profiles 
AUTOSAR specifies a set of standardized and configurable End-2-End profiles, 
which implement a set of protection mechanisms and specify the data format for the 
attached End-2-End header. 
An End-2-End Profile uses a subset of the following data protection mechanisms:34 
1. CRC Checksum, provided by the CRC library; 
2. Sequence Counter incremented at every transmission request, the value is 
checked at receiver side for correct incrementation; 
3. Alive Counter incremented at every transmission request, the value checked at 
the receiver side if it changes at all, but correct incrementation is not checked 
4. A specific ID for every port data element sent over a port (global to system, where 
the system may contain potentially several ECUs). 
5. Timeout detection: Receiver communication timeout and Sender 
acknowledgement timeout  
 
 Three End-2-End Profiles are specified in the AUTOSAR Standard, Profile 1 with two 
variants, End-2-End Profile 2 and End -2-End Profile 4. Upcoming releases will also 
specify Profiles 5 and 6. 
Only the standardized End -2-End profiles shall be used, non -standard End -2-End 
Profile configurations may only be used in special cases, such as for legacy software.

Only the standardized End -2-End profiles shall be used, non -standard End -2-End 
Profile configurations may only be used in special cases, such as for legacy software.  
The protection mechanisms of the End-2-End Profile 1 are described in Table 8 as 
follows:

Request. This Value is explicitly sent.

determine if the value of the counter has been

which is used in the CRC calculation. The CRC 
calculation is illustrated in Figure 14. 
The Data ID value is not explicitly sent. As the ID is

user_input: Given that the RAM Test module cannot ensure data consistency during NMI, DMA transfers, and in a multicore system, how does the ECU Manager's coordination of multi cores impact the execution of RAM tests during the power-up, sleep, and shutdown phases?
reference: The ECU Manager's coordination of multi cores ensures that the necessary conditions for executing RAM tests are met during the power-up, sleep, and shutdown phases. Since the RAM Test module requires a controlled environment where no other software or hardware modifies the RAM area under test, the ECU Manager's management of these states helps to prevent concurrent modifications by other cores, thus maintaining data consistency during the execution of RAM tests.
reference_contexts:
Detection and Reaction 
During the execution of non -destructive tests, the RAM Test module saves the contents 
of the RAM area under test and restores the original contents thereafter. 
RAM Test reports errors to the diagnostic event manager (DEM) as production errors. 
 
4.2.4  Limitations 
1. Transient faults are not covered by RAM Test. 
RAM Test can be used to detect static hardware errors during power-up and at 
runtime. Transient faults and intermittent faults are not covered and cannot be 
reliably detected by RAM Test. 
2. During the execution of a RAM test algorithm, no other software and hardware 
shall be allowed to modify the RAM area under test The RAM Test module cannot 
ensure data consistency (e.g. during NMI, DMA transfers, multiple active cores in 
a Multicore system). Therefore the execution of RAM Test may be limited to the 
power-up/sleep/shutdown phase of a microcontroller. 
3. Destructive tests cause corruption of contents in memory under test. 
During the execution of destructive tests, the contents of RAM area under test are 
not saved by the RAM Test module.

ECU State Manager 
The ECU Manager module is a basic software module that manages common 
aspects of ECU states. Specifically, the ECU Manager module: 
  Initializes and de -initializes the OS, the SchM and the BswM as well as some 
basic software driver modules. 
  Configures the ECU for SLEEP and SHUTDOWN when requested. 
  Manages all wakeup events on the ECU. 
  Coordinates multi cores of an ECU. 
 
5.1.4.12 BSW Manager 
Its responsibility is to arbitrate mode requests from application layer SW -Cs or other 
BSW modules based on simple rules, and perform actions based on the arbitration 
result.

user_input: Given the E2E library's capability to support ASIL D communication in AUTOSAR CP, what are the implications for designing a parallel processing system in AUTOSAR AP where the hardware only supports up to ASIL B?
reference: The E2E library in AUTOSAR CP ensures that safety-related data can be transmitted up to ASIL D through a QM communication stack. However, in AUTOSAR AP, the parallel processing hardware typically only supports up to ASIL B. This means that while the software can be developed to meet ASIL D requirements, the overall system cannot achieve ASIL D due to the hardware limitations. To address this, designers can either accept the ASIL B level of the parallel processing subsystem and implement a separate ASIL D or higher subsystem for monitoring, or they can duplicate the ASIL B subsystem to achieve ASIL D, although this may be costly.
reference_contexts:
Requirements on E2E Communication Protection 
AUTOSAR CP Release 4.4.0   
 
10 of 15 Document ID 651: AUTOSAR_SRS_E2E 
5.2.3  E2E Library 
E2E library provides a set of safety protocols, in a form of library functions invoked by 
SW-Cs. The protocols shall provide the error detection that is sufficient for 
transmitting safety-related data up to ASIL D, through a QM communication stack. 
It provides: 
1. E2E profiles 1, 2, 4, 5, 6, 7, 11, 22. 
2. E2E state machine 
 
5.2.3.1

Design guidelines for using parallel processing 
technologies on Adaptive Platform 
AUTOSAR AP Release 18-10  
14 of 16 Document ID 884: AUTOSAR_EXP_ParallelProcessingGuidelines 
For accelerator-model, there is still the server process and also the main thread and 
some sub-threads, the main computation will be performed by the kernel executed on 
the accelerator. Although it varies, the accelerator is often only capable of executing 
a single kernel at a time. Therefore, it is not possible to perform the redundant 
execution in parallel, unless the accelerator is capable of running multiple kernels in 
parallel or by employing multiple accelerator units. With a single accelerator, one 
option is to perform the redundant execution in series. However, as this will impact 
the performance, a practical option is to abandon the redundant execution and take 
the system design approach of accepting the service-based parallel processing at 
ASIL-B, and another ASIL-B or higher sub-system for monitoring the result of 
accelerator-model service. 
 4.3 Safety considerations 
Safety is a system design topic. One of the typical issues is that the parallel 
processing hardware technology does not satisfy ASIL-D but only up to ASIL-B. The 
software can be developed based on ASIL-D practices but as the hardware is only 
capable of ASIL-B, as a whole it cannot achieve ASIL-D. The required ASIL for a 
“subsystem” depends on the system functionality it provides – e.g. parallel 
processing subsystem is used for ASIL-B system functionality, which computation 
result is safety-checked by ASIL-D subsystem. Or, one can go duplicate ASIL-B 
subsystem to achieve ASIL-D (though it may be expensive). The design guidelines 
for system design to achieve overall functional safety requirements are out of the 
scope of this document. 
 
To achieve the determinism that is essential for achieving ASIL-D by the service-
based parallel processing, it is advised to follow the approach as discussed in “4.2 
Deterministic execution”.

user_input: How does the handling of deadline timeouts for individual signals in the first use case of IPdu 1 compare with the handling of deadline timeouts for signal groups in the test case involving IPdu 4, particularly in terms of notification to the SWC and the retention of signal values?
reference: In both the first use case of IPdu 1 and the test case involving IPdu 4, the SUT must notify the SWC when a deadline timeout occurs. Additionally, in both scenarios, the value of the affected signals (Signal 1 and Signal 2 in IPdu 1, and Signal Group 2 in IPdu 4) must be retained as the last valid received value.
reference_contexts:
Summary The SUT is started. The Rx IPdu 1, IPdu 2 are sent periodically on the bus, with the 
update-bit of the Signal 1 and Signal 2 set to 1. Two use case must be tested: 
- use case with IPdu 1 (Ignoring IPDU base timeout handling if all signals are 
configured with update-bit): 
  1- After a while, the update-bit of the Signal 1 is set to 0 
  2- After Signal 1 Timeout, the update-bit of the Signal 2 is set to 0 
- use case with IPdu 2 (Smallest configured timeout for signals without update bits):  
  1- After a while, stop sending IPdu 2 
  2- Check Signal 3 and Signal 4 Deadline timeout 
Awaiting result for first use case with IPdu 1 (Ignoring IPDU base timeout handling if 
all signals are configured with update-bit): 
 Deadline timeout occurs independently (with configured timeout value) for 
the Signal 1 and Signal 2. The SUT must notify Deadline timeout to the 
SWC. 
 The value of the Signal 1 and Signal 2 must be the same as the last valid 
received value. 
Awaiting result for first use case with IPdu 2 (Smallest configured timeout for signals 
without update bits): 
 Deadline timeout occurs is the same for the Signal 3 and Signal 4 (Smallest 
configured timeout). The SUT must notify Deadline timeout to the SWC.  
 The value of the Signal 3 and Signal 4 must be the same as the last valid 
received value.

user_input: How does the error reporting mechanism described in the AUTOSAR standard for the NVRAM manager (NvM) interact with the recovery actions specified for read and write operations, and what are the implications for the design of an application software component (SWC) that needs to handle these errors?
reference: The error reporting mechanism in the AUTOSAR standard for the NVRAM manager (NvM) interacts with the recovery actions for read and write operations by notifying the user of errors if the recovery mechanisms fail. For read operations, recovery actions include 'read retry', 'read redundant block', and 'read rom block'. For write operations, the recovery action is 'write retry'. If these recovery mechanisms fail, the error NVM_E_REQ_FAILED is reported to the DEM, and the error can be polled using NvM_GetErrorStatus or reported via configurable callbacks. The design of an application software component (SWC) that needs to handle these errors can either poll the job status using NvM_GetErrorStatus or provide a server runnable attached to a NvMNotifyJobFinished server port, which is invoked by the NVM upon job completion.

user_input: Given the 8-bit CRC used in E2E Profile 2 and the 16-bit CRC used in E2E Profile 6, how do the different lengths of the CRC polynomials impact the detection of masquerading faults in each profile, and what implications does this have for the design of the DataIDList in Profile 2?
reference: The 8-bit CRC used in E2E Profile 2 has a limited detection capability for masquerading faults, which means such faults may not be detected in a single communication cycle. Instead, the detection relies on the adequate selection of Data IDs from the DataIDList over successive cycles. In contrast, the 16-bit CRC used in E2E Profile 6 provides a higher detection rate and sufficient Hamming Distance, allowing for more robust detection of masquerading faults within a single cycle. This implies that the design of the DataIDList in Profile 2 must account for the need to detect faults over multiple cycles, whereas Profile 6 can rely more on the inherent strength of its longer CRC polynomial.
reference_contexts:
CRC Corruption, Asymmetric information 4
Table 6.14: Detectable communication faults using Profile 2
[PRS_E2E_00117]⌈E2E Profile 2 shall use the Crc_CalculateCRC8H2F() function of
the SWS CRC Supervision for calculating CRC checksums. ⌋(RS_E2E_08528)
[PRS_E2E_00118]⌈E2E Profile 2 shall use 0xFF as the start value CRC_StartValue8
for CRC calculation.⌋(RS_E2E_08528)
[PRS_E2E_00119]⌈In E2E Profile 2, the specific Data ID used to calculate a specific
CRC shall be of length 8 bit. ⌋(RS_E2E_08528)
[PRS_E2E_00120]⌈In E2E Profile 2, the specific Data ID used for CRC calculation
shall be selected from a pre-defined DataIDList[16] using the value of the Counter as
an index.⌋(RS_E2E_08528)
Each data, which is protected by a CRC owns a dedicated DataIDList which is de-
posited on the sender site and all the receiver sites.
The pre-defined DataIDList[16] is generated offline. In general, there are several fac-
tors influencing the contents of DataIDList, e.g:
1. length of the protected data
2. number of protected data elements
3. number of cycles within a masquerading fault has to be detected
4. number of senders and receivers
5. characteristics of the CRC polynomial.
Due to the limited length of the 8bit polynomial, a masquerading fault cannot be de-
tected in a specific cycle when evaluating a received CRC value. Due to the adequate
Data IDs in the DataIDList, a masquerading fault can be detected in one of the succes-
sive communication cycles.
Due to the underlying rules for the DataIDList, the system design of the application has
to take into account that a masquerading fault is detected not until evaluating a certain
number of communication cycles.

6.7.5 CRC
E2E Profile 6 uses a 16-bit CRC, to ensure a sufficient detection rate and sufficient
Hamming Distance.
[PRS_E2E_00420]⌈ E2E Profile 6 shall use the Crc_CalculateCRC16() function of the
SWS CRC Supervision for calculating the CRC (Polynomial: 0x1021; Autosar nota-
tion).⌋(RS_E2E_08528, RS_E2E_08539)
[PRS_E2E_00421]⌈ In E2E Profile 6, the CRC shall be calculated over the entire E2E
header (excluding the CRC bytes), including the user data extended with the Data ID.
⌋(RS_E2E_08539, RS_E2E_08536)

enable to check the validity of received data element, when the receiver is executed

Data Elements or respectively I-PDUs, but instead if the receiver reads the currently

user_input: How does the data type determination for the number of axis points in a map (as described in the macro encapsulation concept) influence the generation of the parameters for the interpolation routine in the RTE, particularly for ImplementationDataTypes of category STRUCTURE?
reference: The data type for the number of axis points in a map, which is determined to be the same as the data type of the x-axis, influences the generation of parameters for the interpolation routine in the RTE. Specifically, when generating the typedef and structure for an ImplementationDataType of category STRUCTURE, the RTE ensures that the data type for the number of axis points is consistent with the x-axis data type. This consistency is crucial for avoiding fill bytes and ensuring efficient memory usage, as the generated structures must accurately represent the data types of the elements they encapsulate.
reference_contexts:
Macro Encapsulation of Interpolation Calls 
AUTOSAR CP Release 4.4.0  
 
25 of 32 Document ID 808: AUTOSAR_EXP_MacroEncapsulationofInterpolationCalls 
7.3.1.9  Data Type of the Number of Axis Points 
The macro encapsulation concept does not need this data type explicitly but the 
interpolation routine applies a special data type for the parameter for the number of 
axis points. Additionally the number of axis point is an element which is located in 
memory as well as the axis and values of a curve or map. Therefore the data type for 
the number of axis points has to be defined when the ImplementationDataType is 
derived from an ApplicationDataType. 
The rule to determine the data type for the number of axis points is quite easy: 
The number of axis points gets the same data type as the first axis. 
 
 Impacts for curves: 
A curve has only one axis. Therefore the number of axis points gets the 
same data type as the x axis. If the x axis is a sint8 axis the number of axis points will 
be of data type sint8 too. It is clear that negative numbers of axis points makes no 
sense but 127 axis points should be sufficient. If the axis is from uint8, sint16 or 
uin16 type the number of axis points use the same data types too. 
 
 Impacts for maps: 
A map has two axes. Here the number of axis points of the x and y 
axes gets the data type of the x axis. The reason for this is to avoid fill bytes within 
definition of ImplementationDataType. To understand this point further a definition 
has to be made.

Macro Encapsulation of Interpolation Calls 
AUTOSAR CP Release 4.4.0  
 
30 of 32 Document ID 808: AUTOSAR_EXP_MacroEncapsulationofInterpolationCalls 
7.3.2.3 Generation of the Parameters of the Interpolation Routine for 
ImplementationDataType of Category STRUCTURE 
As decided in the concept decision in chapter 7.3.1.2 input variables for the curve or 
map interpolation are not encapsulated. In general they are available over 
DataAccess.dataDefProperties.swCalprmAxisSet.variableRef. 
Only the parameters for the number of axis points, pointer to the axis and pointer to 
the curve or map values are generated. To get these parameters RTE generated 
information is used. 
 
The RTE generates typedefs and structures depending on ImplementationData-
Types which are the based on SwRecordLayouts of the corresponding curves or 
maps. The Macro Encapsulation Generator Tool has to know the same methods like 
the RTE to derive a typedef and structure from an ImplementationDataType to be 
able to use that information. 
 
By default the RTE generates for each ImplementationDataType with category 
attribute set to "STRUCTURE" following typedef in the RTE Data Type header file 
"Rte_Type.h". This is done in the "RTE Contract" and "RTE Generation" phase. 
 
typedef struct { <elements> } <name>; 
 
where <elements> is the record element specification and <name> is the 
shortName of the Structure Implementation Data Type. For each record element 
defined by one ImplementationDataTypeElement one record element specification 
<elements> is defined. The record element specifications are ordered according the 
order of the related ImplementationDataTypeElements in the input configuration. 
Sequent record elements are separated with a semicolon. It is ensured by RTE that 
the names of the structure and their elements are unique. The prefix Rte_ is not used 
because the type names representing AUTOSAR Data Types.

user_input: How does the Type Length (TYLE) specified in the Log and Trace Protocol affect the data payload length for signed (SINT) and unsigned (UINT) data types as described in the protocol specification?
reference: The Type Length (TYLE) is a 4-bit field that specifies the length of the standard data type. For signed (SINT) and unsigned (UINT) data types, the Type Length can be 1 (8 bit), 2 (16 bit), 3 (32 bit), 4 (64 bit), or 5 (128 bit). This value directly determines the length of the data payload, which can be 8, 16, 32, 64, or 128 bits, respectively.

user_input: How does the presence of the 'Variable Info' (VARI) flag in the Type Info field affect the structure of the Data Payload for both String (STRG) and Array (ARAY) types according to the AUTOSAR Log and Trace Protocol Specification?
reference: When the 'Variable Info' (VARI) flag is set in the Type Info field, the Data Payload for both String (STRG) and Array (ARAY) types includes additional fields. For the String (STRG) type, the Data Payload includes a 16-bit unsigned integer specifying the length of the name and a null-terminated string representing the name of the variable. For the Array (ARAY) type, the Data Payload includes the name of the array, which is described when VARI is set. Both types require the inclusion of these additional fields to provide metadata about the variable or array being logged.

user_input: How does the execution time protection mechanism described in the Operating System specification interact with the fail-silent behavior required for software partitioning in the context of mixed-criticality applications on a single ECU?
reference: The execution time protection mechanism, which monitors the upper bound for task execution times, works in conjunction with the fail-silent behavior required for software partitioning. When a task exceeds its execution budget, the operating system can terminate the offending task to prevent it from affecting other partitions. This ensures that timing errors do not propagate, maintaining the integrity and safety of mixed-criticality applications on a single ECU.
reference_contexts:
Execution Time Protection. An upper bound for execution time of Tasks or Cat224 
Interrupts, the so called Execution Budget, is monitored via the OS to prevent 
timing errors. 
2.  Locking Time Protection. An upper bound for blocking of resources, locking and 
suspending of interrupts, the so called Lock Budget, is monitored by the OS. 
3.  Inter-Arrival Time Protection. A lower bound between tasks being activated or Cat 
2 Interrupts arriving, a so called Time Frame, is monitored via the OS to prevent 
timing errors. 
 
Note: Execution time enforcement requires hardware support, e.g. a timing 
enforcement interrupt. If an interrupt is used to implement the time enforcement, the 
priority of this interrupt shall be high enough to “interrupt” the supervised tasks or 
interrupts. 
                                                                                                                                        
22 Specification of Watchdog Manager, V2.5.0, R4.1 Rev 3, Page 61, Chapter 7.3 Watchdog Handling  
23 Specification of Operating System, V5.3.0 R4.1 Rev 3, Chapter 7.7.2 
24 Category 2 Interrupts are managed by the Operating System. Category 1 Interrupts are executed 
outside of the Operating System and therefore cannot be monitored.

Use Case 1: Software Partitioning [UC1] 
Software partitioning is used to prevent the effects of faults in one partition from 
spreading to other partitions on the same ECU, by specifying well defined boundaries 
in the space and time dimensions for the softw are of the partitions to execute within. 
By terminating a partition, so called fail silent  [16] behaviour can be implemented. 
Additionally, this is an important aspect for building safety cases and for liability 
considerations when mixed-criticality applications execute on one ECU. See also the 
OS SWS [3] for more information. Commonly used examples of such techniques are 
memory partitioning and execution time (deadline) monitoring. 
Memory partitioni ng and execution time monitoring can be used to detect memory 
access errors and timing errors, and to prevent them from corrupting software in 
other partitions. However, when such errors occur, a reaction from the system is 
necessary. The most straightforw ard approach is to not allow the offending software 
to continue its execution, i.e., to forcibly terminate it. This will prevent errors from 
propagating but it may put the system in an inconsistent state, since the software in 
the partition may have been i nteracting with software in other partitions and/or BSW 
at the time of termination. The behaviour of the system and its consistency rules 
must therefore be well specified, such that a partition can continue to execute without 
inadvertent disturbance from a sudden termination or restart of another partition.

user_input: Given the constraints of the Classic Platform (CP) in supporting Ethernet-based communications and the increasing need for high-performance processors, what implications does this have for the design of the Adaptive Platform (AP) in terms of processor architecture and communication efficiency?
reference: The limitations of the Classic Platform (CP) in supporting Ethernet-based communications and high-performance processors imply that the Adaptive Platform (AP) must adopt a more advanced and scalable design. Specifically, the AP should incorporate heterogeneous computing architectures, including manycore processors, GPUs, FPGAs, and accelerators, to meet the growing processing demands. Additionally, the AP must leverage efficient communication technologies such as Network-on-Chip (NoC) to facilitate high-speed, low-latency communication between processing elements, thereby addressing the inefficiencies of legacy inter-ECU communications.
reference_contexts:
Technology Drivers 
There are two major groups of technology drivers behind. One is  Ethernet, and the 
other is processors. 
 
The ever-increasing bandwidth requirement of the on-vehicle network has led to the 
introduction of Ethernet, that offers higher bandwidth and with switched networks, 
enabling more efficient transfer of long messages, point-to-point communications, 
among others, compared to the legacy in-vehicle communication technologies such 
as CAN. The CP, although it supports Ethernet, is primarily designed for the legacy 
communication technologies, and it has been optimized for such, and it is difficult to 
fully utilize and benefit from the capability of Ethernet-based communications. 
 
Similarly, performance requirements for processors have grown tremendously in 
recent years as vehicles are becoming even more intelligent. Multicore processors 
are already in use with CP, but the needs for the processing power calls for more 
than multicore. Manycore processors with tens to hundreds of cores, GPGPU 
(General Purpose use of GPU), FPGA, and dedicated accelerators are emerging, as

Explanation of Adaptive Platform Design 
AUTOSAR AP Release 18-10  
 
9 of 81 Document ID 706: AUTOSAR_EXP_PlatformDesign 
these offer orders of magnitudes higher performance than the conventional MCUs. 
The increasing number of cores overwhelms the design of CP, which was originally 
designed for a single core MCU, though it can support multicore. Also, as the 
computing power swells, the power efficiency is already becoming an issue even in 
data centers, and it is in fact much more significant for these intelligent ECUs. From 
semiconductor and processor technologies point of view, constrained by Pollack’s 
Rule, it is physically not possible to increase the processor frequency endlessly and 
the only way to scale the performance is to employ multiple (and many) cores and 
execute in parallel. Also, it is known that the best performance-per-watt is achieved 
by a mix of different computing resources like manycore, co-processors, GPU, 
FPGA, and accelerators. This is called heterogeneous computing – which is now 
being exploited in HPC (High-Performance Computing) - certainly overwhelms the 
scope of CP by far. 
 
It is also worthwhile to mention that there is a combined effect of both processors and 
faster communications. As more processing elements are being combined in a single 
chip like manycore processors, the communication between these processing 
elements is becoming orders of magnitude faster and efficient than legacy inter-ECU 
communications. This has been made possible by the new type of processor inter-
connect technologies such as Network-on-Chip (NoC). Such combined effects of 
more processing power and faster communication within a chip also prompts the 
need for a new platform that can scale over ever-increasing system requirements. 
2.3

user_input: Given the constraints on the counter in E2E Profile 11 as described in the E2E Supervision, what is the maximum allowed difference between the counter value of the current message and the previous valid message, and how does this impact the detection of data repetition and errors?
reference: The maximum allowed difference between the counter value of the current message and the previous valid message in E2E Profile 11 is defined by the parameter maxDeltaCounter. This parameter impacts the detection of data repetition and errors: if the counter increment is within the allowed limits, it indicates that some data may have been lost but the sequence is still considered valid (OK). If the counter increment exceeds the maxDeltaCounter, it indicates an error, meaning too many data elements were lost.
reference_contexts:
6.9.2 Counter
In E2E Profile 11, the counter is initialized, incremented, reset and checked by E2E
profile. The counter is not manipulated or used by the caller of the E2E Supervision.
[PRS_E2E_00504]⌈ In E2E Profile 11, on the sender side, for the first transmission re-
quest of a data element the counter shall be initialized with 0 and shall be incremented
by 1 for every subsequent send request. When the counter reaches the maximum
value (0x0E), then it shall restart with 0 for the next send request. ⌋(RS_E2E_08539)
Note that the counter value 0x0F is reserved as a special invalid value, and must never
be used by the E2E profile 11.
In E2E Profile 11, on the receiver side, by evaluating the counter of received data
against the counter of previously received data, the following is detected:
1. Repetition:
a. no new data has arrived since last invocation of E2E Supervision check func-
tion,
b. the data is repeated
2. OK:
a. counter is incremented by one (i.e. no data lost),
b. counter is incremented more than by one, but still within allowed limits (i.e.
some data lost),
3. Error: a. counter is incremented more than allowed (i.e. too many data lost).
Case 1 corresponds to the failed alive counter check, and case 3 correspond to failed
sequence counter check.

Parameters Profile Description
dataID 1, 4, 5,
6, 7, 11
This represents a unique numerical identifier. Note: ID is used for protection
against masquerading. The details concerning the maximum number of values
(this information is specific for each E2E profile) applicable for this attribute are
controlled by a semantic constraint that depends on the category of the EndT oEnd-
Protection.
dataId is used as a unique identifier of a configuration object. One dataId can
appear only once in the configuration.
profileName all This represents the identification of the concrete E2E profile. Possible profiles: 1
(only CP), 2 (only CP), 4, 5, 6, 7, 11, 22.
dataLength 1, 2, 5,
11, 22
For fixed size data: length of data in bits.
minDataLength 4, 6, 7 For variable size data: minimum length of data in bits.
maxDataLength 4, 6, 7 For variable size data: maximum length of data in bits.
dataIdList 2, 22 List of 16 dataID values, where a a different value is transmitted depending on
counter value.
dataIdMode 1, 11 This attribute describes the inclusion mode that is used to include the two-byte
Data ID in E2E communication protection.
offset 2, 4, 5,
6, 7, 22
Offset of the E2E header in the Data[] array in bits.
counterOffset 1, 11 Offset of the counter in the Data[] array in bits.
crcOffset 1, 11 Offset of the CRC in the Data[] array in bits.
dataIdNibbleOffset 1, 11 Offset of the dataID nibble in the Data[] array in bits.
maxDeltaCounter 4, 5, 6,
7, 11, 12
Maximum allowed difference between the counter value of the current message
and the previous valid message.

user_input: How does the safety concept on vehicle level outlined in AUTOSAR CP Release 4.4.0 interact with the specific hazard and risk assessment outcomes, particularly regarding the total loss of low beam (ASIL B) and its fault tolerance time?
reference: The safety concept on vehicle level in AUTOSAR CP Release 4.4.0 provides a framework for the OEM to define and communicate safety requirements to suppliers. In the context of the hazard H1, which is the total loss of low beam rated ASIL B, the safety goal SG01 aims to prevent this hazard. The fault tolerance time of 500 ms ensures that the system must activate the low beam within this timeframe to avoid a hazardous situation, especially under critical conditions such as night driving on unlit roads.
reference_contexts:
Safety Use Case Example 
AUTOSAR CP Release 4.4.0   
12 of 61 Document ID 641: AUTOSAR_EXP_SafetyUseCase 
3  Safety Concept on Vehicle Level 
The following chapter outlines the safety analysis on vehicle level and its result 
typically created by the OEM and partially provided to a supplier. 
 
Note: This structure of safety analysis cannot be mapped 1 by 1 to the structure in 
ISO 26262  [1], since the norm does not include the different possible scopes of 
development, explicitly. 
 
3.1

Outcome of Hazard Analysis and Risk Assessment 
For this example, it is supposed the following hazard and attributes were identified as 
output of a hazard and risk assessment:  
 
Hazard H1: Total loss of low beam (ASIL B)  
 
This hazard potentially could cause the driver  to lose control of the vehicle, leave the 
road and collide with environmental parts.  
 
Exceptions and Boundary Conditions to H1:  
 The loss of low beam is only to be seen as a risk in case of bad viewing 
conditions (night, fog, etc.). 
 The loss of low beam on a curvy, unlighted rural road is evaluated as a most 
critical situation. 
 The loss of only one low beam is not considered to be directly le ading to a 
hazardous situation.  However, it is a latent fault and will be included in the 
concept advisement.   
 
ASIL: The ASIL B rating is based on the severity rate the exposure rate and the 
controllability identified during hazard analysis and risk assessment. 
 
Safety Goal SG01: Prevent total loss of low beam 
 
Safe State: Low beam activated 
 
Fault Tolerance Time: 500 ms  
(Loss of low beam during night drive with activated lights shall be considered.) 
 
Note: Additional hazards could be assumed. However, to limit our example  it is 
assumed to be the only relevant hazard. For the following discussion of AUTOSAR 
features it seems not to be helpful to include additional risks at this point in time. 
 
3.2

user_input: How does the configuration of the Rx signal group deadline timeout with action NONE (as described in Context 1) influence the behavior of the SWC when it reads the initial value of Signal_3_1 after a deadline timeout (as described in Context 2)?
reference: When the Rx signal group deadline timeout is configured with action NONE, the system does not perform any specific action upon the timeout. As a result, when the SWC reads the value of Signal_3_1 after a deadline timeout, it receives the initial value that was configured, as demonstrated in the test steps where the initial FLOAT32 value is provided to the SWC despite no frame being sent to the SUT.

user_input: How does the ApmcParamConfContainerDef class, which defines the structure of parameter and reference containers on the 'definition' side, relate to the ApmcContainerValue class, which represents the corresponding container on the 'value' side, in the context of the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform's machine configuration model?
reference: The ApmcParamConfContainerDef class defines the structure of parameter and reference containers on the 'definition' side, while the ApmcContainerValue class represents the corresponding container on the 'value' side. The relationship between these classes is established through the 'definition' attribute in ApmcContainerValue, which references ApmcContainerDef, the superclass of ApmcParamConfContainerDef. This allows the 'value' side to reflect the structure defined on the 'definition' side, enabling the creation of nested container structures in the machine configuration model.

user_input: How does the definition of the default value for a float parameter in ApmcFloatParamDef interact with the constraints imposed by the max and min limits in the same class?
reference: The default value for a float parameter in ApmcFloatParamDef is defined by the attribute 'defaultValue'. This default value applies if the parameter is instantiated without assigning a specific value. However, the actual value of the parameter, including the default value, must adhere to the constraints set by the 'max' and 'min' attributes, which define the upper and lower limits, respectively. Therefore, the default value must fall within the range specified by these limits.

user_input: Given that the IPv6 SD Endpoint Option specifies the IPv6-Address, transport layer protocol, and Port Number of the sender, and the transport layer protocol for SOME/IP-SD is currently set to 0x11 (UDP) with a port number of 30490, what are the implications for the receiving SD implementation when processing an SD message containing this option?
reference: When the receiving SD implementation processes an SD message containing the IPv6 SD Endpoint Option, it must use the IPv6-Address, transport layer protocol (0x11, UDP), and Port Number (30490) specified in the option instead of the Source IP Address and Source Port from the message header for responding to the SD message.

user_input: What is the difference in the configuration parameters for providing an initial value if the Rx signal was never received for a UINT8 signal compared to a SINT8 signal in the AUTOSAR communication bus test cases?
reference: The configuration parameters for providing an initial value if the Rx signal was never received differ in the data type specified for the signal. For a UINT8 signal, the ComSignalType is set to UINT8, while for a SINT8 signal, the ComSignalType is set to SINT8. Both configurations require that the ComSignalInitValue is configured and different from 0.

user_input: How does the buffer handling mechanism differ between the first transformer in a transformer chain and subsequent transformers, and what are the implications for the design of the bufferLength parameter in each case?
reference: The first transformer in a transformer chain receives the operation arguments and optionally the return value as input, and outputs a uint8 array containing the transformed data. The buffer handling for the first transformer can be either in-place or out-of-place, depending on configuration. For in-place transformation, the buffer is both an input and output parameter, while for out-of-place transformation, the buffer is only an output parameter. Subsequent transformers in the chain receive the output of the previous transformer as input, and the buffer handling follows the same in-place or out-of-place mechanism. In both cases, the bufferLength parameter is used to inform the RTE of the actual length of the transformed data, which may be smaller than the maximum buffer size allocated.
reference_contexts:
General Specification of T ransformers
AUTOSAR CP Release 4.4.0
 Description: This function is the interface of the first transformer in a transformer
chain of Client/Server communication. It takes the operation arguments
and optionally the return value as input and outputs a uint8 array
containing the transformed data.
The length of the transformed data shall be calculated by the transformer
during runtime and returned in the OUT parameter bufferLength. It may
be smaller than the maximum buffer size used by the RTE for buffer
allocation.
General Specification of T ransformers
AUTOSAR CP Release 4.4.0
 Parameters (inout): buffer This argument is only an INOUT argument for trans-
formers which are not configured for out-of-place
transformation. It is the buffer where the input data
are placed by the RTE and which is filled by the
transformer with its output. If executeDespiteDataU-
navailability is set to true and the RTE cannot pro-
vide data as input to the transformer, it will hand over
a NULL pointer to the transformer.
 Parameters (out): buffer This argument is only an OUT argument for trans-
formers configured for out-of-place transformation.
It is the buffer allocated by the RTE, where the trans-
formed data has to be stored by the transformer.
bufferLength Here, the transformer informs the Rte how large the
output data really were. It is possible that the length
of the output is shorter than the maximum buffer size
allocated.
 Return value: uint8 0x00 (E_OK): T ransformation successful
0x01 - 0xff: Specific errors
Description: This function is the interface of a transformer which is not the first
transformer in a transformer chain. It takes the output of an earlier
transformer in the chain and transforms the data.
The length of the transformed data shall be calculated by the transformer
during runtime and returned in the OUT parameter bufferLength. It may
be smaller than the maximum buffer size used by the RTE for buffer
allocation.

user_input: How does the requirement for the target of an ApmcForeignReferenceValue to exist within the M2 model scope contrast with the flexibility provided by ApmcUriForeignReferenceValue, where the target does not need to exist in the current model scope?
reference: ApmcForeignReferenceValue requires the target model element to exist within the M2 model scope, ensuring that the referenced element is present and resolvable. In contrast, ApmcUriForeignReferenceValue allows references to model elements that do not necessarily exist in the current model scope, providing flexibility by treating the existence of the reference itself as an identifying characteristic of the ApmcContainerValue that owns it.

user_input: Given the requirement for AUTOSAR to ensure data consistency across multiple cores (RS_BRF_02040), what are the implications for the design of the Flash Driver (Fls) when it is bound to a single core and does not require multi-core functionalities?
reference: The requirement for AUTOSAR to ensure data consistency across multiple cores (RS_BRF_02040) implies that when the Flash Driver (Fls) is bound to a single core, it does not need to implement multi-core functionalities for data consistency. Since the Fls operates within a single core, it inherently avoids the complexities of ensuring consistent access to shared data across multiple cores, thus simplifying the design and reducing the need for mechanisms like mutual exclusion or atomic sequences.
reference_contexts:

user_input: How does the memory partitioning mechanism described in the AUTOSAR OS specification impact the generation of glue code for ASIL-to-ASIL calls when the caller and callee are in different ASIL partitions?
reference: The memory partitioning mechanism in the AUTOSAR OS ensures that OS-Applications are isolated from each other, preventing write access to private data sections and stacks. When generating glue code for ASIL-to-ASIL calls between different ASIL partitions, this isolation necessitates the use of static glue code or vendor-specific configuration parameters to manage the communication, as the standard configuration mechanisms may not suffice due to the lack of BSW module and EcuConfiguration information for integrator code.
reference_contexts:
Overview of Functional Safety Measures in AUTOSAR 
AUTOSAR CP Release 4.4.0  
13 of 96 Document ID 664: AUTOSAR_TR_OverviewOfFunctionalSafetyMeasuresInAUTOSAR 
 
2.1.2.4  Memory Partitioning within Application Software 
Application Software in an AUTOSAR ECU can consist of safety -related and non -
safety-related Software Component s.  Freedom from interference between Software 
Components with different ASIL ratings shall be ensured according to the 
requirements of ISO 262629. 
 
The AUTOSAR Operating System provides freedom from interference for memory -
related faults by placing OS -Applications into separate memory regions. This 
mechanism is called Memory Partitioning. OS -Applications are protected from each 
other, as code executing in the Memory Partition of one OS -Application cannot 
modify other memory regions. The corresponding requi rements from the AUTOSAR 
OS specification are presented in Table 1. 
 
Req. ID Requirement Text 
[SWS_Os_ 
00207] 
The Operating System module shall prevent write access to the OS -
Application’s private data sections from other non -trusted OS -
Applications. 
[SWS_Os_ 
00355] 
The Operating System module shall prevent write access to all private 
stacks of Tasks/Category 2 ISRs of an OS -Application from other non -
trusted OS-Applications. 
[SWS_Os_ 
00356] 
The Operating System module shall pre vent write access to all private 
data sections of a Task/Category 2 ISR of an OS -Application from other 
non-trusted OS-Applications. 
Table 1:

3.2.3.3 ASIL calls ASIL 
The case of an ASIL to ASIL call can be seen as a combination of 3.2.3.2 and 
3.2.3.1. Also here a generic glue code might be needed if the modules are not placed 
in the same ASIL partition. In this case either the caller or the callee have to provide 
this glue code. In an ASIL system the glue code is normally provided by those 
modules which have the higher ASIL. The glue code can be created statically or can 
be generated.  
 
For the generation of the glue code the following limitations exist: 
 
 Calls to integrator code: A configuration via SchM_Call() is not possible since the 
integrator code does not belong to any BSW module and does not have any 
configuration (EcuConfiguration) and module (BSWDT) information which could 
be used. In such cases 
o Either a static glue code has to be used to encapsulate calls from/to 
integrator code and the integrator code might need small changes, e.g. 
changing the name of the called function to avoid name clashes.  
o or offer vendor specific configuration parameter which holds per callout a 
reference to the OsApplication where the integration code is placed. 
 
 If we know only the address of the callee (this can happen if the interface is 
generic and function pointers are used for the call, e.g. in the PDU Router) we 
need a dedicated vendor specific configuration parameter for the ASIL module 
which provides the information in which partition the callee is located.

user_input: How does the configuration of RxCrcValidated affect the validation process of the CRC for the AUTOSAR TL V Sub-TL V fields, particularly when MessageCompliance is set to FALSE?
reference: When MessageCompliance is set to FALSE, the configuration of RxCrcValidated affects the validation process of the CRC for the AUTOSAR TL V Sub-TL V fields as follows: If RxCrcValidated is set to CRC_IGNORED, any CRC inside the Sub-TL Vs is ignored. If set to CRC_NOT_VALIDATED, Ethernet discards Follow_Up messages with Sub-TL Vs of Type 0x28, 0x44, 0x50, or 0x60. If set to CRC_OPTIONAL, Ethernet discards Follow_Up messages with these Sub-TL Vs that contain an incorrect CRC value. If set to CRC_VALIDATED, Ethernet discards Follow_Up messages with these Sub-TL Vs that contain an incorrect CRC value and also rejects messages with Sub-TL Vs of Type 0x34, 0x51, or 0x61.

user_input: In the context of E2E Profiles 2 and 22, how does the process of calculating the CRC differ when the Data ID is included in the calculation, and what specific steps are involved in each profile?
reference: In E2E Profile 2, the CRC is calculated over Data[1], Data[2], ..., Data[Config->DataLength/8-1] of the data buffer, extended with the Data ID. The Data ID is selected from DataIDList[] based on the Counter value. In E2E Profile 22, the CRC calculation involves two steps: first, the CRC is computed over &Data[1] with a length of Length-1, starting with 0xFF. Then, the Data ID from Config->DataIDList[Counter] is appended, and the CRC is recalculated using the previously computed CRC as the start value.
reference_contexts:
E2E Protocol Specification
AUTOSAR FO Release 1.5.0
[PRS_E2E_00130]⌈ In E2E Profile 2, the E2E_P02Protect() function shall update the
Counter (i.e. low nibble (Bit 0...Bit 3) of Data byte 1) in the data buffer (Data) after
incrementing the Counter.⌋(RS_E2E_08528)
The specific Data ID used for this send request is then determined from a DataIDList[]
depending on the value of the Counter (Counter is used as an index to select the Data
ID from DataIDList[]). The DataIDList[] is defined in E2E_P02ConfigT ype.
[PRS_E2E_00132] ⌈ In E2E Profile 2, after determining the specific Data ID,
the E2E_P02Protect() function shall calculate the CRC over Data[1], Data[2], ...
Data[Config->DataLength/8-1] of the data buffer (Data) extended with the Data ID. ⌋
(RS_E2E_08528)
[PRS_E2E_00133] ⌈ In E2E Profile 2, the E2E_P02Protect() function shall up-
date the CRC (i.e. Data[0]) in the data buffer (Data) after computing the CRC. ⌋
(RS_E2E_08528)
The specific Data ID itself is not transmitted on the bus. It is just a virtual message key
used for the CRC calculation.

user_input: How does the configuration of the Rx signal group deadline timeout with action NONE (as described in ATS_COMINDEP_00107) affect the provision of the initial value to the SWC when the Rx IPdu_1 is never sent to the SUT (as described in ATS_COMINDEP_00082)?
reference: When the Rx signal group deadline timeout is configured with action NONE, the system does not perform any specific action upon the timeout. In the scenario where the Rx IPdu_1 is never sent to the SUT, the SWC still receives the initial value of the signal (UINT32) as specified in the test steps. This indicates that the configuration with action NONE allows the initial value to be provided to the SWC even when the expected IPdu is not received, ensuring that the SWC has a defined state despite the timeout.
