user_input: How do the error reporting mechanisms for EEPROM write failures and EEPROM erase failures differ in terms of the specific error codes used and the methods by which the errors are communicated to the EEPROM Abstraction layer?
reference: For EEPROM write failures, the error is reported to the DEM with the error code EEP_E_WRITE_FAILED, while for EEPROM erase failures, the error is reported with the error code EEP_E_ERASE_FAILED. In both cases, the error is communicated to the EEPROM Abstraction layer either by polling with the function Eep_GetJobResult (job result set to MEMIF_JOB_FAILED) or by the callback function Fee_JobErrorNotification.

user_input: Given the configuration parameters specified in the Acceptance Test Specification for Communication via bus (Context 1), how does the requirement for the ComRxDataTimeoutAction set to REPLACE influence the behavior described in the test steps of Context 2, particularly in Step 5 where the SWC checks the value of Signal_3_1 after the Rx IPdu_3 is stopped?
reference: The ComRxDataTimeoutAction set to REPLACE ensures that when the Rx IPdu 3 is stopped and a timeout occurs, the signal value is replaced with its initial value. In Step 5 of the test, after the Rx IPdu 3 is stopped, the SWC reads the value of Signal_3_1 and finds it to be the initial value, which is different from 0, as configured.

user_input: How does the explicit synchronization mechanism described in the NvM Block Descriptor configuration interact with the interfaces NvMAdmin, NvM_NotifyInitBlock, and NvM_NotifyJobFinished to ensure proper data management during read and write operations in an AUTOSAR application?
reference: The explicit synchronization mechanism in the NvM Block Descriptor configuration ensures that data is managed properly during read and write operations by coordinating with the NvMAdmin, NvM_NotifyInitBlock, and NvM_NotifyJobFinished interfaces. When the NvMBlockUseSyncMechanism parameter is set to true, the NvM module uses callbacks to synchronize data. During a write operation, the application SW-C calls NvM_WriteBlock, which triggers the NvM module to invoke the NvMWriteRamBlockToNvCallback. This callback copies the data from the application data area to the NvM Ram Mirror. Similarly, during a read operation, the NvMReadRamBlockFromNvCallback updates the application data area from the NvM module's mirror. The NvM_NotifyInitBlock and NvM_NotifyJobFinished interfaces provide notifications to the application SW-C about the initialization and completion of these operations, ensuring that the application is aware of the status and can manage its data accordingly.

Synchronization) 
NvM Block Descriptor is required to be configured to have explicit synchronization for 
this use case (NvMBlockUseSyncMechanism parameter set to true).  
 
In this scenario, data is maintained locally by the application SW -C. A call to 
NvM_WriteBlock will start the process but the data can be modified till that point in 
time when a callback referred by configuration parameter 
NvMWriteRamBlockToNvCallback occurs. The data is copied from the application 
data area to the NvM Ram Mirror as part of this callback. Similarly, during read 
operation, ap plication data area is updated from NvM module's mirror via callback 
configured using NvMReadRamBlockFromNvCallback parameter.

user_input: How does the master/satellite pattern described in the AUTOSAR BSW architecture influence the design and implementation of the immobilizer system's inter-domain interfaces, particularly in terms of API consistency and vendor-specific solutions?
reference: The master/satellite pattern in AUTOSAR BSW architecture ensures that the master and satellites provide consistent APIs, allowing them to be treated as a single module entity. This consistency is crucial for the immobilizer system's inter-domain interfaces, where the central locking master and other components must communicate seamlessly. Since both the master and satellites are vendor-specific solutions, they maintain the same interfaces as specified in AUTOSAR, facilitating the integration of the immobilizer system into partitioned systems without requiring changes to other modules.
reference_contexts:
Master/Satellite-approach  
Modules that need to be accessed in different BSW partitions can be implemented 
using the master/satellite pattern.  
The distribution of work between master and satellite is implementation specific. One 
extreme is that the satellite only provides the interfaces to the other modules in the 
same BSW partition, and that it routes all requests to the master and answers back 
to the other modules. At the other extreme, the satellite can provide the full 
functionality locally (e.g. local mode management for a complete application which 
runs in the same BSW partition) and only synchronizes its internal states with the 
master, if necessary. There might even be several masters for different functionality, 
e.g. two PduR masters for a distributed PduR gateway. 
The master coordinates requests from the satellites and can filter or monitor 
incoming satellite requests. The master and one or several satellites are treated like 
being one module entity in some respect: 
 Master and satellites are always vendor specific solutions, coming from the same 
vendor. 
 The interfaces of master and satellite to other module entities in general are the 
same as specified in AUTOSAR for traditional modules. Master and satellite 
should provide the same APIs. This means that when migrating to partitioned 
systems, existing module entities can be replaced by a master and one or several 
satellites, in most cases without changing other modules. Exceptions might be 
module internal adaptations to additional delays which are caused by inter-
partition communication.

user_input: How does the registration mechanism described in the configuration philosophy of AUTOSAR CP Release 4.4.0 ensure that the CRYIF can effectively manage multiple CRYPTO modules, and what are the implications for key storage in both hardware and software CRYPTO implementations?
reference: The registration mechanism in AUTOSAR CP Release 4.4.0 ensures that the CRYIF can manage multiple CRYPTO modules by loading a pre-configuration provided by the vendor, which represents the capabilities of each CRYPTO module. This pre-configuration is loaded from the CRYPTO layer to the CRYIF and then to the CSM, allowing the CRYIF to mount and integrate the cryptographic features of each registered CRYPTO module into its generic interface. For key storage, a hardware CRYPTO implementation typically uses key slots, while a software CRYPTO implementation uses the Non-Volatile Memory (NvM) to manage key storage. This ensures that the CRYIF can effectively handle different key storage mechanisms based on the type of CRYPTO module.
reference_contexts:
The Crypto Interface (CRYIF) 
The CRYIF is enclosed by the upper layer CSM and by the lower layer CRYPTO. It 
receives requests from the CSM and maps them to the appropriate cryptographic 
operation in the CRYPTO. CRYIF forwards the requests given by CSM to the 
particular CRYPTO. Callback notifications inform about the outcome in case the 
request was asynchronous. 
 
The CRYIF can operate several CRYPTO modules. There could be, for example, 
one CRYPTO module for an external cryptographic hardware module and one 
CRYPTO module holding a cryptographic software library. The CRYIF provides a

Utilization of Crypto Services 
AUTOSAR CP Release 4.4.0   
 
12 of 13 Document ID 602: AUTOSAR_EXP_UtilizationOfCryptoServices 
element index definition [3]. For example, the crypto service MAC has a mandatory 
key element “Key Material” with key element id “1”. The key elements indices can be 
extended by the vendor. A key element can also be part of several key types. 
 
During key configuration the reference to an appropriate key type has to be specified. 
The particular CRYPTO provides data storage for the key elements contained in the 
key type, where the actual key data is actually held. A HW CRYPTO implementation 
will typically use key slots. Whereas a SW CRYPTO will use the NvM for managing 
the key storage. 
 
 
 5.3 Configuration Philosophy 
 5.3.1 Registration of Crypto Functionality 
The concept of arbitrarily many CRYPTO modules enables flexibility regarding 
available cryptographic features. This requires a registration mechanism that makes 
the actual cryptographic features in the CRYPTO implementations known to the 
CRYIF and CSM. Otherwise, it would not be possible to configure and use the actual 
cryptographic features in the CSM. 
 
This is solved by a pre-configuration provided by the vendor, which represents the 
capabilities of each CRYPTO module. The pre-configuration is loaded from bottom to 
top, i.e. starting from CRYPTO over CRYIF to CSM. CRYIF mounts each registered 
CRYPTO module and integrates the particular cryptographic features into the generic 
interface provided to the CSM. The CSM takes over the available cryptographic 
features of the registered CRYPTO and deduces the particular cryptographic 
features for configuration by the user.

user_input: Given that the BSW modules in AUTOSAR have limited access to hardware when memory protection is enabled, how would you design the Free Running Timer module to ensure it can still perform its required hardware access while adhering to the memory protection constraints?
reference: To ensure the Free Running Timer module can perform its required hardware access while adhering to memory protection constraints, the CDD approach can be used. This involves creating a small piece of code that accesses the necessary hardware and mapping this code to a trusted OsApplication with memory protection disabled. The Free Running Timer module would then call this code whenever it needs to access the hardware, thereby maintaining the integrity of the memory protection while allowing the required hardware operations to occur.
reference_contexts:
Guide to BSW Distribution 
AUTOSAR CP Release 4.4.0  
54 of 64 Document ID 631:  AUTOSAR_EXP_BSWDistributionGuide 
 
Note that the above example is quite typical for AUTOSAR inter-partition calls. It 
assumes that the lifetime of the buffer is equal to the duration of the called function. If 
this is different, e.g. one function which just provides a buffer and another function at 
a later time indicate that the buffer is now ready (example: NvM read mechanism) an 
adoption is needed. 
 
 3.2.4 Access to peripherals / hardware  
In AUTOSAR the access to peripherals or hardware is limited to BSW modules. 
Typically only some of them require a real access, e.g.: 
- The Os switches between different contexts and need to read/write the 
context registers. Also interrupt locking requires normally access to 
hardware registers or execution of privileged instructions. 
- During startup the Mcu driver needs to enable the microcontroller clocks 
and may perform further initialization of registers 
- IO drivers need to access their part of the hardware. 
- … 
If parts of the BSW are now running in a partition where the memory protection is 
enabled the full access to hardware is normally no longer possible. In such cases a 
hardware access can be realized by: 
 1. “CDD approach”: Create a piece of code which access the required hardware 
and map this code to a trusted OsAppication with memory protection disabled. 
This allows the code to have full access. From within your BSW module all 
hardware access must then call this small piece of code. In this case this code 
has full access to hardware. 
Requirements on Free Running Timer 
AUTOSAR CP Release 4.4.0   
 
8 of 19 Document ID 211: AUTOSAR_SRS_FreeRunningTimer 
4  Functional Overview 
 
This chapter describes the requirements on functionality of the module Free Running 
Timer. Chapter 4.1 introduces SWFRT by an overview, 4.2 and 4.3 contain the 
requirements. The functionality will be accessed by Low Level SW as well as by 
application. Therefore the location in the SLA needs to be in the services area (SLA 
ID: 02-06).

user_input: How does the format of the IPv6 Endpoint Option specified in the SOME/IP Service Discovery Protocol impact the identification of the SOME/IP-SD Instance when the IP-Address and Port Number cannot be used, as described in the IPv6 SD Endpoint Option?
reference: The format of the IPv6 Endpoint Option includes fields such as the IPv6-Address and Transport Protocol Port Number, which are used to transport the endpoint information of the sender's SD implementation. When the IP-Address and Port Number cannot be used, the IPv6 SD Endpoint Option ensures that the SOME/IP-SD Instance can still be identified, even in scenarios like proxy service discovery where one ECU handles multicast traffic for another.
reference_contexts:
SOME/IP Service Discovery Protocol Specification
AUTOSAR FO Release 1.5.0
[PRS_SOMEIPSD_00315]⌈ The Format of the IPv6 Endpoint Option shall be as fol-
lows:
• Length [uint16]: Shall be set to 0x0015.
• T ype [uint8]: Shall be set to 0x06.
• Discardable Flag [1 bit]: Shall be set to 0.
• Bit 1 to bit 7 are reserved and shall be 0.
• IPv6-Address [uint128]: Shall transport the unicast IP-Address as 16 Bytes.
• Reserved [uint8]: Shall be set to 0x00.
• T ransport Protocol (L4-Proto) [uint8]: Shall be set to the transport layer protocol
(ISO/OSI layer 4) based on the IANA/IETF types (0x06: TCP , 0x11: UDP).
• T ransport Protocol Port Number (L4-Port) [uint16]: Shall be set to the transport
layer port(e.g. 30490).
⌋(RS_SOMEIPSD_00006, RS_SOMEIPSD_00010)
[PRS_SOMEIPSD_00317]⌈
SOME/IP Service Discovery Protocol Specification
AUTOSAR FO Release 1.5.0
 IPv6 SD Endpoint Option
The Ipv6 SD Endpoint Option is used to transport the endpoint (i.e. IP-Address and
Port) of the senders SD implementation. This is used to identify the SOME/IP-SD
Instance even in cases in which the IP-Address and/or Port Number cannot be used.
SOME/IP-SD IPv6 SD Endpoint Option is shown in Figure 4.14
 Note:
A use case would be a proxy service discovery on one ECU which handles the multi-
cast traffic for another ECU.
Figure 4.14: SOME/IP-SD IPv6 SD Endpoint Option

reference: ApmcUriForeignReferenceValue serves as an identifying characteristic of the ApmcContainerValue that owns it, even if the referenced model element does not exist in the current model scope. In contrast, ApmcUpstreamDocForeignReferenceValue is intended for documentation purposes only and does not carry any semantic meaning, regardless of whether the referenced model element exists outside the target-configuration.

user_input: How does the error reporting mechanism described in the NVRAM Manager's reaction to job failures (Context 1) interact with the AUTOSAR standard error reporting requirements for NVM functions (Context 2)?
reference: The error reporting mechanism in the NVRAM Manager's reaction to job failures involves incrementing a write retry counter and, if the retries are exhausted, reporting the error NVM_E_REQ_FAILED to the DEM. This aligns with the AUTOSAR standard error reporting requirements, which specify that if recovery mechanisms fail, the same error NVM_E_REQ_FAILED must be reported to the DEM. Both contexts also describe that the error can be polled by the user using NvM_GetErrorStatus or reported via configurable callbacks SingleBlockCallbackFunction or MultiBlockCallbackFunction.
reference_contexts:
6.2.1.2.5 NVRAM Manager 
Detection Depending on the stack configuration: 
 Polling of the job result MEMIF_JOB_FAILED by the function 
MemIf_GetJobResult (see Memory Abstraction Interface above). 
 Notification by FEE with the callback function 
NvM_JobErrorNotification (see Flash Eeprom emulation above). 
Reaction [SWS_NvM_00213] [SWS_NvM_00296] Increment write retry counter. 
If the number of retries is exceeded, the request is aborted. 
Report  [SWS_NvM_00213] [ SWS_NvM_00296] If recovery actions abort, 
the error NVM_E_REQ_FAILED is reported to the DEM. 
Depending on the configuration 
 [SWS_NvM_00451] [ SWS_NvM_00213] [ SWS_NvM_00296] The 
error shall be polled by the user with the function 
NvM_GetErrorStatus (job result set to NVM_REQ_NOT_OK). 
 [SWS_NvM_00113] [SWS_NvM_00260] The error shall be reported 
to the user via the configurable callbacks 
SingleBlockCallbackFunction or MultiBlockCallbackFunction. 
Recovery [SWS_NvM_00168] [ SWS_NvM_00213] [ SWS_NvM_00296] The 
NVRAM Manager controls the error recovery mechanism. The

user_input: Given that the Fixed Point (FIXP) bit is set, how does the selection of Type Length (TYLE) affect the representation and interpretation of the offset field in the data payload?
reference: When the Fixed Point (FIXP) bit is set, the selection of Type Length (TYLE) determines the size of the offset field. If TYLE equals 4, the offset field is a 64-bit signed integer, and if TYLE equals 5, the offset field is a 128-bit signed integer. This affects the range and precision of the offset value, which is used in the calculation of the logical value from the physical value using the equation log_v = phy_v * quantization + offset.
reference_contexts:
Bit Fixed Point (FIXP) 
 
 
If fixed point values are used, the Fixed Point (FIXP) bit shall be set. Than the Data 
field represents the physical value of a fixed point variable. 
For interpreting the fixed point variable the logical value of this variable has to be 
calculated.  
The logical value is calculated by the physical value, the quantization and the offset 
of fixed point variable.  
 
[PRS_Dlt_00389] ⌈The following equation defines the relation between the logical 
value (log_v) and the physical value (phy_v), offset and quantization: 
log_v = phy_v * quantization + offset ⌋ (RS_LT_00044)

Type Length (TYLE) shall be set to 1, 2, 3, 4 or 5. ⌋ 
(RS_LT_00044) 
 
[PRS_Dlt_00357] ⌈If Variable Info (VARI) is set, the "Length of Name", "Length of 
Unit", the "Name" and the "Unit" fields shall be added. ⌋ (RS_LT_00044) 
 
[PRS_Dlt_00412] ⌈If FIXP is set, the Quantization and Offset fields shall be added. ⌋ 
(RS_LT_00044)

(RS_LT_00044)

TYLE equals 4 the Offset field shall be a 64 singed integer field and if the TYLE 
equals 5 the Offset field shall be a 128 singed integer field. ⌋ (RS_LT_00044)

user_input: How does the configuration of NvData ports in Table 1 reflect the benefits of using NvM as NvBlockSwComponent, particularly in terms of RAM block allocation and sharing between multiple SW-C’s?
reference: The configuration in Table 1 demonstrates the benefits of using NvM as NvBlockSwComponent by showing how RAM blocks are allocated and shared among multiple software components (SW-C’s). For example, NVBD_1 is shared between SWC2 and SWC3, where different parts of the same RAM block are accessed through partial data mapping mechanisms. This allows SWC2 to read and write specific bits (NvD_8bit_0.b(0) and NvD_8bit_0.b(4:1)) and SWC3 to read and write other parts (NvD_16bit_0 and NvD_8bit_0.b(0)). This setup ensures that each block is identified and has a dedicated RAM block, while also enabling independent and efficient data sharing among multiple SW-C’s.
reference_contexts:
Accessing NV Data using NvBlockSwComponent 
Here NvM is configured as  NvBlockSwComponent in RTE and can be utilized to 
create RAM Blocks (mirrors) of its own which can be written/read partially or 
completely by a single or multiple SW-C’s using a NV-Data Interface. 
 
Along with this, the Client -Server Interfaces are used for the NvM services and

user_input: Given the requirement to continuously monitor the availability of services as described in the ara::com API, how does the integrator ensure that the correct instance of the RadarService is monitored when multiple instances of the same service are instantiated within different nesting levels in an executable?
reference: To ensure the correct instance of the RadarService is monitored, the integrator must use the unique instance IDs assigned to each instantiation of the service within different nesting levels. For example, in the context of the B instantiation nested inside the composite component, the RadarService is provided via SOME/IP binding with instance ID '1'. For the B instantiations at the top level, the RadarService is provided via local IPC with instance IDs '/tmp/Radar/3' and '/tmp/Radar/4'. These unique IDs allow the ara::com API to distinguish between different instances of the same service and correctly monitor their availability.
reference_contexts:
Explanation of ara::com API
AUTOSAR AP Release 18-10
35 * continuously monitor the availability of the services
36 * and call the handler on any change.
37 *
38 * \param instanceId which instance of the service type defined
39 * by T shall be searched/found.
40 *
41 * \return a handle for this search/find request, which shall
42 * be used to stop the availability monitoring and related
43 * firing of the given handler. (\see

Figure 8.9: InstanceIds in Deployment
The figure above outlines the "problem" with a simple example. Within Executable
2 there are three instantiations of SoftwareComponentType B implementation in
different contexts (nesting levels). All instances do provide a specific instance of SI
RadarService. The integrator, who applies the Service Instance Manifest for
Process 2 has to do the technical mapping on ara::com level. I.e. he has to de-
cide, which technical transport binding is to be used in each of the B instantiations and
subsequently also, which technical transport binding specific instance ID. In our exam-
ple, the integrator wants to provide the SI RadarService via SOME/IP binding and an
SOME/IP specific instance ID "1" in the context of the B instantiation, which is nested
inside the composite component on the right side, while he decides to provide the SI
RadarService via local IPC (Unix domain socket) binding and a Unix domain socket
specific instance ID "/tmp/Radar/3" and "/tmp/Radar/4" in the context of the B instanti-
ations on the left side, which are not nested (they are instantiated at "top-level" of the
executable). Here it gets obvious, that within the Service Instance Manifest, which al-
lows to specify the mapping of port instantiations within a Process to technical bindings
and their concrete instance IDs, the sole usage of the port name from the model isn’t
sufficient to differentiate. T o get unique identifiers within an executable (and therefore a
process), the nature of nested instantiation and re-use of SoftwareComponentTypes
has to be considered. Every time a SoftwareComponentType gets instantiated, its
instantiation gets a unique name within its instantiation context. This concept applies
to both: C++ implementation level and AUTOSAR meta-model level! In our concrete
example this means:
• B instantiations on top level get unique names on their level: "B_Inst_1" and
"B_Inst_2"

user_input: How does the use of a CompuMethod with category SCALE_LINEAR_AND_TEXTTABLE for data conversion affect the explicit mapping requirements between RPort and PPort table elements in a conversion block?
reference: When using a CompuMethod with the category SCALE_LINEAR_AND_TEXTTABLE for data conversion, the internal representation is calculated using a linear formula for certain ranges, and an explicit mapping is required for each individual value in the text table. This means that in a conversion block, each RPort table element must be explicitly mapped to a corresponding PPort table element to ensure accurate data conversion.
reference_contexts:
Linear Data Conversion 
If the elements of two interfaces are logically equivalent but the range and resolution 
are different, then the linear conversion factor can be calculated out of the semantical 
information of the elements.  In this case the data  semantics is described using a 
CompuMethod with category IDENTICAL, LINEAR, SCALE_LINEAR  or 
SCALE_LINEAR_AND_TEXTTABLE, where the 
 IDENTICAL category means that the value of the physical representation is 
equal to the internal representation and the 
 LINEAR, SCALE_LINEAR or SCALE_LINEAR_AND_TEXTTABLE categories 
mean that the internal representation is calculated out of the physical 
representation by means of a linear formula (factor * external value + offset) 
per range in one or more ranges (SCALE_LINEAR only). 
 
[EXP_Vfb_00141] ⌈ A conversion bloc k involving either IDENTICAL, LINEAR, 
TEXTTABLE, SCALE_LINEAR or SCALE_LINEAR_AND_TEXTTABLE  data types 
shall use the COMPU -METHODS for the respective data types to determine the 
conversion function.⌋ ()

3.9.1.3 Data Mapping 
In case the data semantics is described using a list of values (CompuMethod with 
category TEXTTABLE ) or partially described using a list of values (CompuMethod 
with category SCALE_LINEAR_AND_TEXTTABLE), then an explicit mapping needs 
to be given for each individual value. 
[EXP_Vfb_00142] ⌈ A conversion block involving TEXTTABLE or 
SCALE_LINEAR_AND_TEXTTABLE data types shall use explicit mapping of each 
RPort table element to a PPort table element.⌋ ()

user_input: How does the memory partitioning mechanism described in the AUTOSAR OS specification ensure freedom from interference between OS-Applications with different ASIL ratings, and what are the limitations of this mechanism when multiple Software Components with different ASIL ratings are assigned to the same OS-Application?
reference: The memory partitioning mechanism in the AUTOSAR OS ensures freedom from interference by placing OS-Applications into separate memory regions, preventing write access to private data sections and stacks of tasks/Category 2 ISRs from other non-trusted OS-Applications. However, this mechanism does not provide freedom from interference between Software Components with different ASIL ratings if they are assigned to the same OS-Application, as a faulty Software Component can still modify memory areas of other Software Components within the same OS-Application.
reference_contexts:
Overview of Functional Safety Measures in AUTOSAR 
AUTOSAR CP Release 4.4.0  
13 of 96 Document ID 664: AUTOSAR_TR_OverviewOfFunctionalSafetyMeasuresInAUTOSAR 
 
2.1.2.4  Memory Partitioning within Application Software 
Application Software in an AUTOSAR ECU can consist of safety -related and non -
safety-related Software Component s.  Freedom from interference between Software 
Components with different ASIL ratings shall be ensured according to the 
requirements of ISO 262629. 
 
The AUTOSAR Operating System provides freedom from interference for memory -
related faults by placing OS -Applications into separate memory regions. This 
mechanism is called Memory Partitioning. OS -Applications are protected from each 
other, as code executing in the Memory Partition of one OS -Application cannot 
modify other memory regions. The corresponding requi rements from the AUTOSAR 
OS specification are presented in Table 1. 
 
Req. ID Requirement Text 
[SWS_Os_ 
00207] 
The Operating System module shall prevent write access to the OS -
Application’s private data sections from other non -trusted OS -
Applications. 
[SWS_Os_ 
00355] 
The Operating System module shall prevent write access to all private 
stacks of Tasks/Category 2 ISRs of an OS -Application from other non -
trusted OS-Applications. 
[SWS_Os_ 
00356] 
The Operating System module shall pre vent write access to all private 
data sections of a Task/Category 2 ISR of an OS -Application from other 
non-trusted OS-Applications. 
Table 1:

AUTOSAR OS - Memory Partitioning for OS-Applications10 
 
Application Software can consist of Software Components with different ASIL ratings.  
However, Software Components with different ASIL ratings should not be assigned to 
the same OS -Application. Memory Partitioning does not provide freedom from 
interference between Software Components which  are assigned to the same OS -
Application. The Operating System only prevents other OS -Applications from 
performing improper accesses. A faulty Software Component would not be prevented 
from modifying memory areas of other Software Components within the sam e OS-
Application. 
 
Note: Please consult the subsequent section for details on  Task-level partitioning.  
                                            
9 9 [ISO 26262-6 7.4.10] 
10 Specification of Operating System, V5.3.0 R4.1 Rev 3

user_input: How does the memory partitioning mechanism described in the AUTOSAR OS specification for ensuring freedom from interference between OS-Applications with different ASIL ratings impact the configuration of core-specific ASIL and QM partitions in a multi-core system?
reference: The memory partitioning mechanism in the AUTOSAR OS ensures that OS-Applications with different ASIL ratings are isolated from each other, preventing write access to private data sections and stacks. In a multi-core system, this isolation is extended to core-specific partitions, where BSW is assigned to core-specific QM and ASIL partitions. Each core has its own set of QM and ASIL partitions, and the configuration parameters for these partitions must be set independently to meet both multi-core and safety requirements, ensuring that the memory protection mechanisms are maintained across cores.

The feature can be enabled for each trusted OsApplication via the configuration 
parameter OsTrustedApplicationDelayTimingViolationCall. 
 
3.2.8  Combining Safety and Multi-Core 
In case ASIL systems are implemented using a multi-core architecture, all 
considerations made until now for both, safety and multi-core, are valid. In a multi-
core system, the BSW is assigned to core specific partitions. If safety is added, we 
have core specific QM partitions (one per core) and core specific ASIL partitions. The 
specific multi-core configuration parameters and the specific safety configuration 
parameters are independent and need to be set according to the multi-core 
respectively safety needs.  
 
3.2.9

user_input: How does the role of the FolwCtrlAndAArbn SW-Component in arbitrating between follow control and free cruise control, as described in the AUTOSAR CP Release 4.4.0, align with the functional decomposition of the CrsCtrlAndAcc SW-Composition?
reference: The FolwCtrlAndAArbn SW-Component, as part of the CrsCtrlAndAcc SW-Composition, is responsible for calculating the target control value of vehicle acceleration based on ACC-relevant objects and arbitrating between the target acceleration values from the follow control and free cruise control functionalities. This aligns with the functional decomposition of the CrsCtrlAndAcc SW-Composition, where the FolwCtrlAndAArbn component is specifically tasked with these roles to ensure coherent and safe vehicle control.
reference_contexts:
CrsCtrlAndAcc SW-Composition is decomposed into SW-Components: 
 AccSnsrDataFusion (ACC Sensor Data Fusion) 
 AccObjTarSeln (ACC Target Object Selection) 
 CrsCtrlFree (Free Cruise Control) 
 FolwCtrlAndAArbn (Follow Control and Acceleration Arbitration) 
 CrsCtrlAndAccStCtrl (Cruise Control and ACC State Machine) 
 CrsCtrlAndAccObsvrForVehSt (Cruise Control and ACC Vehicle State Ob-
server) 
This decomposition, shown in Figure 9, enables the realization of variable business 
cases between an OEM and a supplier with different border of responsibility on the 
functionalities inside the CrsCtrlAndAcc SW-Composition.

Explanation of Application Interfaces of the Chassis 
Domain 
AUTOSAR CP Release 4.4.0   
28 of 46 Document ID 270: AUTOSAR_EXP_AIChassis 
 4.1.4 FolwCtrlAndAArbn (Follow Control and Acceleration Arbitration) 
The SW-C FolwCtrlAndAArbn has the role to calculate target control value of vehicle 
acceleration according to the behaviour of ACC relevant object(s) in front of the sens-
ing vehicle. The other  role is to arbitrate the target acceleration values, which are 
provided from the following control functionality and from the free cruise control func-
tionality. The tasks to be done in this SW-Component are, for example: 
 Determination of target acceleration for follow control and standstill 
 Arbitration of the follow control and free cruise control 
Note that arbitration between control and driver intervention is handled by the SW-C 
Vlc.

user_input: Given the requirement for a key element 'Key Material' (CRYPTO_KE_MAC_KEY) in the configuration of a MAC job in AUTOSAR CP, how might the design of a service interface in AUTOSAR AP be optimized to minimize communication overhead when processing multiple MAC jobs in parallel?
reference: To minimize communication overhead when processing multiple MAC jobs in parallel in AUTOSAR AP, the service interface can be designed with a coarse granularity. Instead of providing an interface for processing each MAC job individually, a batch processing interface can be implemented to handle multiple MAC jobs at once. This reduces the number of client-server interactions, thereby decreasing communication overhead and improving performance.
reference_contexts:
Utilization of Crypto Services 
AUTOSAR CP Release 4.4.0   
 
13 of 13 Document ID 602: AUTOSAR_EXP_UtilizationOfCryptoServices 
 6 Example  
In the following, the usage of the crypto stack is outlined using the service “Message 
Authentication Code” (MAC). 
 6.1 Configuration in Modules 
The user creates a job with references to the requested MAC crypto primitive and the 
crypto key to be used. He further configures if the job shall be processed 
synchronously (CRYPTO_PROCESSING_SYNC) or asynchronously 
(CRYPTO_PROCESSING_ASYNC). If the job is asynchronous, he also defines the 
queue which shall handle the job. 
 
MAC requires a key that at least references a key element “Key Material” 
(CRYPTO_KE_MAC_KEY). 
Design guidelines for using parallel processing 
technologies on Adaptive Platform 
AUTOSAR AP Release 18-10  
13 of 16 Document ID 884: AUTOSAR_EXP_ParallelProcessingGuidelines 
 4 Non-functional design topics 
 4.1 Performance 
One of the primary purposes of using parallel processing is to achieve higher 
performance. Since “Service-based parallel processing” utilizes the SOA of AP, the 
general performance-related design techniques also apply. 
 4.1.1 Interface granularity and communication overhead 
The granularity of interface is the size of operation unit per API. If the granularity is 
small, the service has many API. The finer the granularity is, the more flexible the 
service is in general, because it will allow the different application to optimize its 
usage. 
 
In SOA, increasing the granularity will increase the communication between the client 
and server in general. Caching mechanisms may circumvent that problem. However, 
in a real-time system such as AP, caching, depending on the architecture and 
implementation increases non-determinism, thus not a convenient choice. 
 
In AP, there are two possible approaches to minimize the communication overhead 
of services. One is to make the service interface as coarse as possible, especially for 
the interface that has a high frequency in its usage. For example, instead of providing 
an interface only for processing one datum, providing another interface for 
processing a batch of data at a time is recommended. The other approach is to 
optimize at the service interface library. This means that the service interface may 
cache some server-side data for client-local processing, and/or simple interfaces that 
set up or read fields in an object stored locally in the client process heap. The two 
techniques can be mixed.

user_input: How does the configuration parameter NvMSetRamBlockStatusApi, when set to TRUE, influence the interaction between the NVRAM Manager and the SWCs listed in the configuration table, particularly in terms of managing the status of RAM blocks and ensuring data integrity during the NvM_WriteAll process?
reference: When NvMSetRamBlockStatusApi is set to TRUE, the NVRAM Manager requires explicit notification from the SWCs whenever the data in a RAM block is modified. For example, in the configuration table, SWC1 modifies NvD_str0_0, which is mapped to NVBD_0.RAMBlock. After any change, SWC1 must call NvM_SetRamBlockStatus with BlockChanged set to TRUE for NVBD_0. Similarly, SWC2 and SWC3 modify different parts of NVBD_1.RAMBlock and must also notify the NVRAM Manager of changes. This ensures that only the modified blocks are processed during the NvM_WriteAll process, optimizing write cycles and maintaining data integrity.
reference_contexts:
NV Data Handling Guideline 
AUTOSAR CP Release 4.4.0   
 
15 of 51    Document ID 810: AUTOSAR_EXP_NVDataHandling 
In order to minimize the number of write c ycles to the NV memory it is useful to only 
copy the content of those RAM Blocks to the corresponding NV Blocks, for which the 
data content has been changed by the NVRAM Block user. In order to enable this 
feature during the NvM_WriteAll process the config uration parameter 
NvMSetRamBlockStatusApi has to be set to the value TRUE. In this case the 
NVRAM Block user has to inform the NVRAM Manager after every change made in 
the RAM Block data the has to be informed by calling the API 
NvM_SetRamBlockStatus for t he corresponding NVRAM Block, with the parameter 
BlockChanged set to TRUE. This way this NVRAM Block will be marked as to be 
processed during the NvM_WriteAll process.

user_input: How does the memory partitioning mechanism described in the AUTOSAR OS specification impact the design of BSW modules that require direct hardware access, and what solutions are available to ensure these modules can still function correctly within a partitioned environment?
reference: Memory partitioning in AUTOSAR ensures that OS-Applications are isolated from each other, preventing write access to private data sections and stacks. This isolation can limit the ability of BSW modules to directly access hardware, as they may run in partitions with memory protection enabled. To address this, one solution is the CDD approach, where a small piece of code is created to access the required hardware and mapped to a trusted OS-Application with memory protection disabled. This allows the BSW module to call this code for hardware access, ensuring that the module can still function correctly within a partitioned environment.
reference_contexts:
Overview of Functional Safety Measures in AUTOSAR 
AUTOSAR CP Release 4.4.0  
13 of 96 Document ID 664: AUTOSAR_TR_OverviewOfFunctionalSafetyMeasuresInAUTOSAR 
 
2.1.2.4  Memory Partitioning within Application Software 
Application Software in an AUTOSAR ECU can consist of safety -related and non -
safety-related Software Component s.  Freedom from interference between Software 
Components with different ASIL ratings shall be ensured according to the 
requirements of ISO 262629. 
 
The AUTOSAR Operating System provides freedom from interference for memory -
related faults by placing OS -Applications into separate memory regions. This 
mechanism is called Memory Partitioning. OS -Applications are protected from each 
other, as code executing in the Memory Partition of one OS -Application cannot 
modify other memory regions. The corresponding requi rements from the AUTOSAR 
OS specification are presented in Table 1. 
 
Req. ID Requirement Text 
[SWS_Os_ 
00207] 
The Operating System module shall prevent write access to the OS -
Application’s private data sections from other non -trusted OS -
Applications. 
[SWS_Os_ 
00355] 
The Operating System module shall prevent write access to all private 
stacks of Tasks/Category 2 ISRs of an OS -Application from other non -
trusted OS-Applications. 
[SWS_Os_ 
00356] 
The Operating System module shall pre vent write access to all private

Guide to BSW Distribution 
AUTOSAR CP Release 4.4.0  
54 of 64 Document ID 631:  AUTOSAR_EXP_BSWDistributionGuide 
 
Note that the above example is quite typical for AUTOSAR inter-partition calls. It 
assumes that the lifetime of the buffer is equal to the duration of the called function. If 
this is different, e.g. one function which just provides a buffer and another function at 
a later time indicate that the buffer is now ready (example: NvM read mechanism) an 
adoption is needed. 
 
 3.2.4 Access to peripherals / hardware  
In AUTOSAR the access to peripherals or hardware is limited to BSW modules. 
Typically only some of them require a real access, e.g.: 
- The Os switches between different contexts and need to read/write the 
context registers. Also interrupt locking requires normally access to 
hardware registers or execution of privileged instructions. 
- During startup the Mcu driver needs to enable the microcontroller clocks 
and may perform further initialization of registers 
- IO drivers need to access their part of the hardware. 
- … 
If parts of the BSW are now running in a partition where the memory protection is 
enabled the full access to hardware is normally no longer possible. In such cases a 
hardware access can be realized by: 
 1. “CDD approach”: Create a piece of code which access the required hardware 
and map this code to a trusted OsAppication with memory protection disabled. 
This allows the code to have full access. From within your BSW module all 
hardware access must then call this small piece of code. In this case this code 
has full access to hardware.

user_input: How does the Time Master's method of sending the 'second' and 'nanosecond' parts of the Offset Time Base value via the OfsTimeSec and OfsTimeNSec elements influence the Time Slave's calculation of the Offset Time Base?
reference: The Time Master sends the 'second' part of the Offset Time Base value via the OfsTimeSec element and the 'nanosecond' part via the OfsTimeNSec element. The Time Slave then calculates the Offset Time Base using these specific elements, ensuring that both the seconds and nanoseconds are accurately synchronized between the Time Master and the Time Slave.

part of the Offset Time Base value via the OfsTimeSec element of the correspond-
ing AUTOSAR Sub-TL V: OFS and the "nanosecond" part of the Offset Time Base
value via the OfsTimeNSec element of the corresponding AUTOSAR Sub-TL V: OFS
⌋(RS_TS_20063)

[PRS_TS_00097] ⌈ The DataID shall be calculated as: DataID =
DataIDList[Follow_Up.sequenceId mod 16] , where DataIDList is given by
configuration for the Follow_Up.⌋(RS_TS_20061)
Note: A specific DataID out of a predefined DataIDList ensures the identification
of data elements of Time Synchronization messages.
[PRS_TS_00182]⌈ If applying the CRC calculation on multibyte values, the byte order
shall be such, that the byte containing the most significant bit of the value shall be used
first.⌋(RS_TS_20061)
[PRS_TS_00184]⌈ If applying the CRC calculation on multibyte message data, the
byte order shall be in ascending order of the octets, i.e., the octet with the lowest offset
shall be used first. ⌋(RS_TS_20061)

user_input: How does the behavior of Rte_Write() when the TP buffer is locked due to an ongoing transmission, as described in Context 1, affect the ability to transmit a second frame after the Transmit Confirmation has been invoked for the ongoing transmission, as described in Context 2?
reference: In Context 1, Rte_Write() returns RTE_E_COM_BUSY when called during an ongoing transmission, indicating that the TP buffer is locked. In Context 2, the test scenario involves invoking the Transmit Confirmation for the ongoing transmission, which unlocks the signal buffer. This allows a second frame to be transmitted from the application after the Transmit Confirmation has been received, demonstrating that the buffer lock is released upon confirmation.

user_input: How do the customizable parameters described in the context of mandatory vs. customizable parts (Context 1) influence the configuration of the User Request frame (DIRECT) + MDT test case (Context 2), particularly in terms of signal initialization and bit position settings?
reference: The customizable parameters such as ComSignalInitValue and ComBitPosition influence the configuration of the User Request frame (DIRECT) + MDT test case. Specifically, ComSignalInitValue sets the initial value of the signal, ensuring it is consistent with the associated data element. ComBitPosition and ComUpdateBitPosition determine the location of the signal within the PDU, which is crucial for the correct transmission of the signal in the DIRECT mode.

be consistent to associated dataElement

values => the location of these elements in the pdu 
 frames identifiers

user_input: How does the concept of a Remote Procedure Call (RPC) in the SOME/IP specification interact with the definition of a service interface in the context of AUTOSAR, particularly in terms of the methods and events it includes?
reference: A Remote Procedure Call (RPC) in the SOME/IP specification involves a method call from one ECU to another, transmitted using messages. In the context of AUTOSAR, a service interface formally specifies a service, including its methods and events. Therefore, the methods and events defined in a service interface can be invoked through RPCs, enabling inter-ECU communication where the client ECU invokes methods on the server ECU, and the server ECU responds with the results of the method invocation.
reference_contexts:
Acronyms and Abbreviations
The glossary below includes acronyms and abbreviations relevant to the SOME/IP
specification that are not included in the [1, AUTOSAR glossary].
Abbreviation / Acronym: Description:
Byte Order Mark
The byte order mark (BOM) is a Unicode character, U+FEFF byte
order mark (BOM), whose appearance as a magic number at the
start of a text stream is used to indicate the used encoding.
Method A method, procedure, function, or subroutine that is called/in-
voked.
Parameters input, output, or input/output arguments of a method or an event
Remote Procedure Call (RPC) A method call from one ECU to another that is transmitted using
messages
Request a message of the client to the server invoking a method
Response a message of the server to the client transporting results of a
method invocation
Request/Response communica-
tion a RPC that consists of request and response
Event
A uni-directional data transmission that is only invoked on
changes or cyclically and is sent from the producer of data to
the consumers.
Field A field does represent a status and thus has an valid value at all
times on which getter, setter and notifier act upon.
Notification Event An event message of the notifier of a field.
Getter A Request/Response call that allows read access to a field.
Setter A Request/Response call that allows write access to a field.
Notifier Sends out event message with a new value on change of the
value of the field.

Service A logical combination of zero or more methods, zero or more
events, and zero or more fields.
 Service Interface the formal specification of the service including its methods,
events, and fields
 Eventgroup A logical grouping of events and notification events of fields inside
a service in order to allow subscription
 Service Instance Implementation of a service, which can exist more than once in
the vehicle and more than once on an ECU
Server The ECU offering a service instance shall be called server in the
context of this service instance.
Client The ECU using the service instance of a server shall be called
client in the context of this service instance.
Fire and Forget Requests without response message are called fire&forget.
User Datagram Protocol A standard network protocol using a simple connectionless com-
munication model.
Union A data structure that dynamically assumes different data types.
non-extensible (standard) struct
A struct which is serialized without tags. At most, new members
can be added in a compatible way at the end of the struct and
optional members are not possible.

user_input: How does the use of Blueprint Mapping Sets, as described in the Application Interfaces User Guide, facilitate the tracing of PortInterfaces used in the PortPrototypes defined in the AI Table’s “06”-sheets?
reference: Blueprint Mapping Sets establish a connection between a Blueprint and the elements derived from it, allowing for the tracing back of the Blueprint used to create these elements. In the context of PortInterfaces, this means that the PortInterfaces used in the PortPrototypes defined in the AI Table’s “06”-sheets can be traced back to their original blueprints, ensuring that the specific PortInterfaces and their configurations can be identified and managed effectively.
reference_contexts:
9.5 PortInterfaces 
The provided or req uired PortPrototype references a PortInterface defined in the AI 
Table’s “06”-sheets, separated for Sender-Receiver- and Client-Server-Interfaces. 
Both sender-receiver-interfaces and client-server-interfaces are kept in the package 
/AUTOSAR/AISpecification/PortInterfaces_Blueprint. They are also part of 
/AUTOSAR/AISpecification/PortInterfaces_Example, but only as a copy of the blueprint so 
that they could be used for PortPrototypes. 
 
 9.5.1 Sender-Receiver-Interface 
Figure 75 shows one interface from the sender -receiver-interface table, which 
defines short -name, long -name, description and the contained data elements (the 
table is capable of capturing up to six data elements per SenderReceiverInterface). 
The direction of the dataflow is defined by the port.

Application Interfaces User Guide 
AUTOSAR CP Release 4.4.0   
 
87 of 102 Document ID 442: AUTOSAR_EXP_AIUserGuide 
For details on the generation of description and introduction elements see Section 
9.2.5. 
 9.6 Blueprint Mapping Sets 
BlueprintMappingSets are used to establish a connection between a Blueprint and 
element derived from this Blueprint.  This helps to trace back the respective Blueprint 
that was used to create this element. Bl ueprint Mapping Sets are used for different 
elements including PortPrototype Blueprints, PortInterfaces, Application DataTypes, 
etc. They are defined in the package  
/AUTOSAR/AISpecification/BlueprintMappingSets_Example. The following xml 
provides an example for the

user_input: How do the general requirements for error handling in AUTOSAR basic software modules, as described in the first context, influence the specific error values defined for the EEPROM driver in the second context?
reference: The general requirement that errors should indicate an error and use a limited set of return values influences the specific error values defined for the EEPROM driver. These values, such as EEP_E_BUSY and EEP_E_PARAM_ADDRESS, are designed to avoid name clashes and clearly show which module they belong to, adhering to the uniform AUTOSAR error values.

user_input: Given that AUTOSAR COM and LargeDataCOM do not support splitting large signals into different I-PDUs, what implication does this have for the placement of dynamic length signals within an I-PDU according to SRS_Com_02093?
reference: Since AUTOSAR COM and LargeDataCOM do not support splitting large signals into different I-PDUs, the dynamic length signal must be placed last in the I-PDU to ensure that the receiving COM can directly derive the length of the dynamic length signal from the length of the I-PDU without requiring additional encoding.
reference_contexts:
6.1.3 Support of Large Data Types 
 
AUTOSAR COM and LargeDataCOM shall support signals larger than the N-PDUs 
of the underlying busses. For these large signals also a dynamic length shall be 
supported. The requirements of this chapter define the support of these data types. 
 
6.1.3.1 [SRS_Com_02091] AUTOSAR COM and LargeDataCOM shall not 
support splitting of large signals into different I-PDUs 
⌈ 
Type: Valid 
Description: Large signals supported by AUTOSAR COM and LargeDataCOM shall 
never be split into different I-PDUs, but it shall be supported that an I-PDU 
can be split into different N-PDUs 
Rationale: The I-PDU shall be transported by the TP. The TP will work on N-PDU 
level, and therefore can fragment the I-DPU into a number of N-PDUs 
Use Case: There is no use-case to have fragmentation on I-PDU level. 
Dependencies: SRS_Com_02092, SRS_Com_02093 
Supporting Material: -- 
⌋ (RS_BRF_01648, RS_BRF_01568,RS_BRF_01544)

6.2.8.2 [SRS_Com_02093] Dynamic length signal must be placed last in I-PDU 
⌈ 
Type: Valid 
Description: The dynamic length signal must be placed last in the dynamic length 
I-PDU. This is the same approach as in [DOC_ISO_GLOS]. 
 
All other signals and update-bits must be packed in front of the dynamical 
length signal. 
Rationale: Placing the signal last in the frame removes the need of coding the 
dynamical length into the IPDU. The receiving COM can directly derive the 
length of the dynamic length signal from the length of the IPDU 
 
Signal groups may still be used to contain (one) dynamic length signal and 
other signals. 
Use Case: see Rationale 
Dependencies: SRS_Com_02091, SRS_Com_02092 
Supporting Material: [DOC_ISO_GLOS] 
⌋ (RS_BRF_01648,RS_BRF_01544) 
 
6.2.8.3 [SRS_Com_02096] The AUTOSAR COM module shall not support 
fragmentation towards the RTE 
⌈ 
Type: Valid 
Description: The AUTOSAR COM module will always send and receive a complete 
signal (no fragmentation supported above TP). 
 
The COM/RTE/application will always receive/transmit complete large 
signals and dynamic length signals. Modules above TP are not involved in

user_input: How does the memory partitioning mechanism described in the AUTOSAR OS specification interact with the requirements for generating glue code when ASIL modules call each other across different ASIL partitions?
reference: The memory partitioning mechanism in the AUTOSAR OS ensures that OS-Applications are isolated from each other, preventing write access to private data sections and stacks. When ASIL modules call each other across different ASIL partitions, the requirement for generating glue code arises to manage these interactions. The glue code must handle the transition between partitions, ensuring that the calling and called modules adhere to the memory protection requirements. This may involve static or dynamically generated glue code, depending on the specific configuration and the need to encapsulate calls to integrator code or manage generic interfaces using function pointers.
reference_contexts:
Overview of Functional Safety Measures in AUTOSAR 
AUTOSAR CP Release 4.4.0  
13 of 96 Document ID 664: AUTOSAR_TR_OverviewOfFunctionalSafetyMeasuresInAUTOSAR 
 
2.1.2.4  Memory Partitioning within Application Software 
Application Software in an AUTOSAR ECU can consist of safety -related and non -
safety-related Software Component s.  Freedom from interference between Software 
Components with different ASIL ratings shall be ensured according to the 
requirements of ISO 262629. 
 
The AUTOSAR Operating System provides freedom from interference for memory -
related faults by placing OS -Applications into separate memory regions. This 
mechanism is called Memory Partitioning. OS -Applications are protected from each 
other, as code executing in the Memory Partition of one OS -Application cannot 
modify other memory regions. The corresponding requi rements from the AUTOSAR 
OS specification are presented in Table 1. 
 
Req. ID Requirement Text 
[SWS_Os_ 
00207] 
The Operating System module shall prevent write access to the OS -
Application’s private data sections from other non -trusted OS -
Applications. 
[SWS_Os_ 
00355] 
The Operating System module shall prevent write access to all private 
stacks of Tasks/Category 2 ISRs of an OS -Application from other non -
trusted OS-Applications. 
[SWS_Os_ 
00356] 
The Operating System module shall pre vent write access to all private 
data sections of a Task/Category 2 ISR of an OS -Application from other 
non-trusted OS-Applications. 
Table 1:

3.2.3.3 ASIL calls ASIL 
The case of an ASIL to ASIL call can be seen as a combination of 3.2.3.2 and 
3.2.3.1. Also here a generic glue code might be needed if the modules are not placed 
in the same ASIL partition. In this case either the caller or the callee have to provide 
this glue code. In an ASIL system the glue code is normally provided by those 
modules which have the higher ASIL. The glue code can be created statically or can 
be generated.  
 
For the generation of the glue code the following limitations exist: 
 
 Calls to integrator code: A configuration via SchM_Call() is not possible since the 
integrator code does not belong to any BSW module and does not have any 
configuration (EcuConfiguration) and module (BSWDT) information which could 
be used. In such cases 
o Either a static glue code has to be used to encapsulate calls from/to 
integrator code and the integrator code might need small changes, e.g. 
changing the name of the called function to avoid name clashes.  
o or offer vendor specific configuration parameter which holds per callout a 
reference to the OsApplication where the integration code is placed. 
 
 If we know only the address of the callee (this can happen if the interface is 
generic and function pointers are used for the call, e.g. in the PDU Router) we 
need a dedicated vendor specific configuration parameter for the ASIL module 
which provides the information in which partition the callee is located.

user_input: How does the initialization and periodic transmission of the IPDU group AT_234_IpduGroup in the test ATS_COMINDEP_00235 compare with the initialization and periodic transmission of the IPDU group AT_227_IpduGroup in the test ATS_COMFR_00228, particularly in terms of the update mechanisms for their respective signals?
reference: In both tests, the IPDU groups are initialized by requesting a ModeSwitch to IPDU_ACTIVATED. For AT_234_IpduGroup, the first TP transmission occurs after OffsetTime, followed by periodic transmissions every PeriodTime. The signal AT_234_Sg1 is initially set to AT_234_Sg1_Value_Init and updated to AT_234_Sg1_Value_1 using Rte_Send(). For AT_227_IpduGroup, the IPDU is sent out after OffsetTime and periodically every PeriodTime. The signal AT_227_Sg1 is initially set to AT_227_Sg1_Value_Init and updated to AT_227_Sg1_Value_1 using Rte_Write(), with the update bit set to 1 during the first send and then reset to 0.

reference: The PortInterfaces_Blueprint package contains all the PortInterface Blueprints, which are then mapped to specific interfaces in the /AUTOSAR/AISpecification/PortInterfaces_Example package through the PortInterfaceBlueprintMappings. user_input: Given the known limitations of the AUTOSAR AP Release 18-10 sensor interface design, how does the introduction of connection time configuration address the need for flexibility in supporting new sensors and interfaces during development?
reference: The introduction of connection time configuration addresses the need for flexibility by allowing new sensors and interfaces to be added or modified without affecting the existing system. This is particularly useful during development, as it enables testing of new interfaces in a working environment without requiring recompilation and re-flashing of the entire system. This approach aligns with the ongoing development of the AUTOSAR AP Release 18-10 sensor interface, which is still evolving and requires adaptability to support new use cases and features.
reference_contexts:
Explanation of Automated Driving Interfaces
AUTOSAR AP Release 18-10
 Known Limitations
This explanatory document may contain assumptions, exemplary items, like exemplary
reference models, use-cases, scenarios, and/or references to exemplary technical so-
lutions, devices, processes or software. Any such assumptions or exemplary items
are contained in this document for illustration purposes only , and they themselves are
not part of the AUTOSAR standard. Neither their presence in such specifications, nor
any later documentation of AUTOSAR conformance of products actually implementing
such exemplary items, imply that intellectual property rights covering such exemplary
items or assumptions are licensed under the same rules as applicable to the AUTOSAR
standard.
List of limitations:
• the list of use cases contains the stable and relevant use cases from the devel-
opment so far, further use cases will be added if needed
• detailed architecture and interface design is still in development
• this document will be extended with the chapter "Interface Design" which explains
the rationale and design choices of the sensor interface
• the description of interface usage is scheduled for later releases
1

Design time configuration was used over decades and a lot of experience exists to
create safe systems. Nevertheless, due to the fact that the interfaces might experience
a high amount of changes during the next years, more flexibility is appreciated. If this
is not introduced in the overall design, workarounds will be created to overcome this
limitation.
Therefore the idea is to use connection time configuration to provide the flexibility that
is mandatory to simplify the development and to avoid workarounds.
Here are some potential future use-cases that show the necessity for flexibility:
• A new sensor supports additionally the last generation interface to act as a space
part.
• A new interface can be added to a sensor without affection the existing system.
This is especially useful during development time when a new interface shall be
tested in a working environment.
• Several new interfaces can be tested in parallel or in tight schedule without re-
compliation and re-flashing of the whole system.
There might also be new features for a sensor that might affect the interface. One
example could be the ultrasonic sensor. Currently the sensor has one beam and the
interface provides the distance to the object. This is used e.g. in the park distance
control. In future, there might be sensors that support more beams. The interface will
be adapted to provide the beam directions and the corresponding distances. Then, it
might be a good idea to calculate a virtual single beam out of the multi beam informa-
tion and to provide this information with the old interface. Then, the sensor supports
the new and the old interface in parallel. A modern automatic parking software will be
adapted to the new interface to use the full capabilities but the park distance control

user_input: How does the ApmcContainerReferenceValue, which references a container within the target-configuration, differ in its application and implications from the ApmcUriForeignReferenceValue, which can reference elements outside the target-configuration, in the context of AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform configurations?
reference: ApmcContainerReferenceValue is used to reference another container within the target-configuration, establishing a link between different parts of the same configuration. In contrast, ApmcUriForeignReferenceValue allows referencing elements outside the target-configuration, such as in the design model, and does not require the target element to exist in the current model scope. This difference implies that ApmcContainerReferenceValue is used for internal configuration linking, while ApmcUriForeignReferenceValue is used for external references, potentially serving as an identifying characteristic even if the target element does not exist.

user_input: Given that the Type Length (TYLE) for a boolean data type (BOOL) is specified as 1 in the Log and Trace Protocol, how does this constraint affect the implementation of the Data Payload when the Variable Info (VARI) is set for a boolean variable in an AUTOSAR module?
reference: When the Type Length (TYLE) for a boolean data type (BOOL) is 1, and Variable Info (VARI) is set, the Data Payload must include the Length of Name, the Name, and the Unit fields. The boolean value itself is represented as an 8-bit unsigned integer, where 0x0 indicates FALSE and 0x1 indicates TRUE. The name and unit fields are encoded in 8-bit ASCII format and are null-terminated.
reference_contexts:
Log and Trace Protocol Specification 
AUTOSAR FO Release 1.5.0 
 
32 of 69 Document ID 787: AUTOSAR_PRS_LogAndTraceProtocol 
5.1.3  Data Types 
 
5.1.3.1  Bits Type Length (TYLE) 
 
 
Type Length specifies the length of the standard data type.  
 
[PRS_Dlt_00354] ⌈Type Length is a bit field of 4 bit.  
 
Type Info contains  
 1 (8 bit) for bool data (BOOL)  
 1 (8 bit) or 2 (16 bit) or 3 (32 bit)  or 4 (64 bit) or 5 (128 bit) for signed 
(SINT) and unsigned integer data (UINT) 
 2 (16 bit) or 3 (32 bit) or  4 (64 bit) or 5 (128 bit) for float data (FLOA) ⌋ 
(RS_LT_00044) 
 
 
5.1.3.2  Bit Variable Info (VARI) 
 
If Variable Info (VARI) is set, the name and the unit of a variable can be added. Both 
always contain a length information field and a field with the text (of name or unit). 
The length field contains the number of characters of the associated name or unit 
filed. The unit information is to add only in some data types. 
 
[PRS_Dlt_00410] ⌈The coding of all text in Variable Info (VARI) shall be in 8-bit 
ASCII format. ⌋ (RS_LT_00025) 
  
[PRS_Dlt_00411] ⌈The strings in VARI shall be null terminated. ⌋ (RS_LT_00044, 
RS_LT_00025) 
 
 
 
5.1.3.3

Type Bool (BOOL) 
 
 
[PRS_Dlt_00422] ⌈If the BOOL bit is set, the Data Payload shall consist of at least 
one 8-bit unsigned integer parameter. ⌋ (RS_LT_00044) 
 
[PRS_Dlt_00423] ⌈If the Data field equals 0x0, it shall be interpreted as FALSE. If 
the Data field equals 0x1 it shall be interpreted as TRUE. ⌋ (RS_LT_00044) 
 
[PRS_Dlt_00139] ⌈Type Length (TYLE) shall be 1. ⌋ (RS_LT_00044) 
 
[PRS_Dlt_00355] ⌈If Variable Info (VARI) is set, the Length of Name, the Name and 
the Unit fields shall be added. ⌋ (RS_LT_00044)

user_input: Given the requirement for deterministic processes in AUTOSAR AP as described in the guidelines, what implications does this have for the design of safety-related functions in the chassis and powertrain domain, as outlined in the project objectives for AUTOSAR FO?
reference: The requirement for deterministic processes in AUTOSAR AP implies that safety-related functions in the chassis and powertrain domain must be designed to ensure predictable and consistent behavior, independent of external factors such as processor load or concurrent thread execution. This is crucial for maintaining functional safety and availability, given the long product life cycles and the need for rapid updates and upgrades in automotive systems.
reference_contexts:
Guidelines for using Adaptive Platform interfaces 
AUTOSAR AP Release 18-10  
10 of 15 Document ID 929: AUTOSAR_EXP_AdaptivePlatformInterfacesGuidelines 
A Process that can be executed fully deterministically must be designed, 
implemented and integrated in a way such that it is independent of processor load 
caused by other functions and calculations, sporadic unrelated events, race 
conditions, deviating random numbers etc. 
 
Non-deterministic behavior may arise from different reasons; for example insufficient 
computing resources, or uncoordinated access of data, potentially by multiple 
threads running on multiple processor cores. The order in which the threads access 
such data will affect the result, which makes it non-deterministic. 
 
Project Objectives 
AUTOSAR FO Release 1.5.0   
9 of 11 Document ID 599: AUTOSAR_RS_ProjectObjectives 
 Rationale: Numerous functions in the automotive domain have requirements on 
functional safety and/or availability. 
Automotive systems are characterized by long product life cycles and 
short reaction times when the need for updates and upgrades comes up. 
Most of the systems in the chassis and powertrain domain are safety 
related.

user_input: Given the requirement EXP_Vfb_00142 for explicit mapping of each RPort table element to a PPort table element when using TEXTTABLE data types, how would you implement the mapping for the 'Transmission Type' COMPU-METHOD, ensuring that each transmission type value is correctly mapped from the internal representation to the physical representation?
reference: To implement the mapping for the 'Transmission Type' COMPU-METHOD, each internal value must be explicitly mapped to its corresponding physical representation. For example, the internal value 0 maps to 'Mt' (manual transmission), 1 maps to 'At' (automatic transmission), 2 maps to 'Amt' (direct shift/automated manual transmission), 3 maps to 'Cvt' (continuously variable transmission), and 4 maps to 'Dct' (twin-clutch gearbox or dual clutch transmission). This ensures that the conversion block adheres to the requirement EXP_Vfb_00142 for explicit mapping of each RPort table element to a PPort table element.

user_input: Given that the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform does not provide ASIL ratings for its architectural items, how does this affect the integration of safety-related functions in the chassis and powertrain domains, which are known to have stringent functional safety requirements?
reference: The absence of ASIL ratings in the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform means that developers must independently assess and integrate safety-related functions in the chassis and powertrain domains. This requires a detailed understanding of the specific safety goals, underlying hardware, and development processes to ensure that the architectural items are combined in a way that meets the stringent functional safety requirements.
reference_contexts:
Explanation of Safety Overview
AUTOSAR AP Release 18-10
 Known Limitations
This explanatory document may contain assumptions, exemplary items, like reference
models, use-cases, scenarios, and/or references to exemplary technical solutions, de-
vices, processes or software. Any such assumptions or exemplary items contained in
this document are for illustration purposes only . These assumptions are not part of the
AUTOSAR standard. Neither their presence in such specifications, nor any later docu-
mentation of AUTOSAR conformance products actually implementing such exemplary
items, imply that intellectual property rights covering such items or assumptions are
licensed under the same rules as applicable to the AUTOSAR standard.
 The chapter 6
• Functional safety concept and initial functional safety requirements
is still in development and open for discussion and should not be considered mature or
final.
The chapters
• T echnical safety concept and technical safety requirements
• Validation of safety requirements, safety analysis and exemplary use-cases
are scheduled for later releases.
 No ASIL Ratings
The AUTOSAR consortium, especially the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform Feature-T eams
are only providing an architecture definition, descriptions of the functional blocks and
a proof of concept implementation, it is not possible to add ASIL ratings to each archi-
tectural item in this scope. It is only possible to give the reader some hints on how to
combine the architectural items to achieve a safe architecture in his own very specific
context: considering the underlying hardware, the products safety goals and metrics
as well as the development processes.
Project Objectives 
AUTOSAR FO Release 1.5.0   
9 of 11 Document ID 599: AUTOSAR_RS_ProjectObjectives 
 Rationale: Numerous functions in the automotive domain have requirements on 
functional safety and/or availability. 
Automotive systems are characterized by long product life cycles and 
short reaction times when the need for updates and upgrades comes up. 
Most of the systems in the chassis and powertrain domain are safety 
related.

user_input: How do the functional safety measures specified in ISO 26262, particularly the restricted coupling between software components, influence the design of the Electronic Parking Brake (EPB) interfaces in the Chassis Domain of AUTOSAR?
reference: The restricted coupling between software components, as specified in ISO 26262, influences the design of the Electronic Parking Brake (EPB) interfaces in the Chassis Domain of AUTOSAR by ensuring that the EPB and other components such as the ESC (Electronic Stability Control) have well-defined and limited interactions. This restriction helps to minimize potential conflicts and faults, thereby enhancing the overall system reliability and safety. The AUTOSAR definition of EPB/ESC interfaces supports all three variants of EPB configurations (stand-alone, master-ESC slave, and ESC master-EPB slave), each designed to maintain clear and controlled interactions, aligning with the functional safety requirements.
reference_contexts:
Explanation of Application Interfaces of the Chassis 
Domain 
AUTOSAR CP Release 4.4.0   
30 of 46 Document ID 270: AUTOSAR_EXP_AIChassis 
SW-C (service brake) and the Epb SW -C (Parking Brake) by sending them braking 
requests. 
 
The motivation to create this Ssm SW -C comes from the fact that on vehicles, these 
“SSM functions” may be included either in the ESC ECU or the EPB ECU.  
 4.4 Software Component Epb (Electronic Parking Brake) 
The SW-C Epb controls the parking brake actuator. Its functions are: 
 
 Realizing the requests from Ssm by controlling the EPB actuator. 
 Interpretation of the driver’s wish for the Chassis Domain when pushing the EPB 
button. 
 
 Current market implementations of the SW-C Epb are comprised of 3 main versions: 
EPB stand-alone: EPB does not work in conjunction with ESC; minimal number of 
interfaces are used to avoid conflicts. 
EPB master-ESC slave: the EPB unit controls the ESC as an actuator. 
ESC master-EPB slave: the ESC controls the EPB actuator; some backup modes are 
implemented at EPB Actuator unit in case of ESC fault. 
 The AUTOSAR definition of EPB/ESC interfaces supports all 3 variants.

user_input: How does the use of a CompuMethod with category SCALE_LINEAR_AND_TEXTTABLE for data conversion impact the explicit mapping required between RPort and PPort table elements in a conversion block?
reference: When using a CompuMethod with the category SCALE_LINEAR_AND_TEXTTABLE, the data conversion involves both a linear transformation and an explicit mapping of individual values. The linear transformation applies a factor and offset to the physical representation to derive the internal representation, while the explicit mapping ensures that each RPort table element is mapped to a corresponding PPort table element. This dual approach ensures that the conversion block accurately handles both the linear scaling and the specific value mappings.
reference_contexts:
Linear Data Conversion 
If the elements of two interfaces are logically equivalent but the range and resolution 
are different, then the linear conversion factor can be calculated out of the semantical 
information of the elements.  In this case the data  semantics is described using a 
CompuMethod with category IDENTICAL, LINEAR, SCALE_LINEAR  or 
SCALE_LINEAR_AND_TEXTTABLE, where the 
 IDENTICAL category means that the value of the physical representation is 
equal to the internal representation and the 
 LINEAR, SCALE_LINEAR or SCALE_LINEAR_AND_TEXTTABLE categories 
mean that the internal representation is calculated out of the physical 
representation by means of a linear formula (factor * external value + offset) 
per range in one or more ranges (SCALE_LINEAR only). 
 
[EXP_Vfb_00141] ⌈ A conversion bloc k involving either IDENTICAL, LINEAR, 
TEXTTABLE, SCALE_LINEAR or SCALE_LINEAR_AND_TEXTTABLE  data types 
shall use the COMPU -METHODS for the respective data types to determine the 
conversion function.⌋ ()

3.9.1.3 Data Mapping 
In case the data semantics is described using a list of values (CompuMethod with 
category TEXTTABLE ) or partially described using a list of values (CompuMethod 
with category SCALE_LINEAR_AND_TEXTTABLE), then an explicit mapping needs 
to be given for each individual value. 
[EXP_Vfb_00142] ⌈ A conversion block involving TEXTTABLE or 
SCALE_LINEAR_AND_TEXTTABLE data types shall use explicit mapping of each 
RPort table element to a PPort table element.⌋ ()

user_input: Given the constraint that the worker pool cannot be used to process multiple different tasks in parallel due to potential heterogeneous runtime utilization, what implications does this have for the design of parallel processing on an AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform where the accelerator can only execute a single kernel at a time?
reference: The constraint that the worker pool cannot process multiple different tasks in parallel due to potential heterogeneous runtime utilization aligns with the limitation of the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform's accelerator, which can only execute a single kernel at a time. Both constraints emphasize the need for careful resource management and deterministic execution to ensure predictable performance and safety. In such a design, parallel processing must be planned to avoid introducing unnecessary complexity and variability in runtime, which could impact the system's ability to meet safety and performance requirements.
reference_contexts:
Design guidelines for using parallel processing 
technologies on Adaptive Platform 
AUTOSAR AP Release 18-10  
14 of 16 Document ID 884: AUTOSAR_EXP_ParallelProcessingGuidelines 
For accelerator-model, there is still the server process and also the main thread and 
some sub-threads, the main computation will be performed by the kernel executed on 
the accelerator. Although it varies, the accelerator is often only capable of executing 
a single kernel at a time. Therefore, it is not possible to perform the redundant 
execution in parallel, unless the accelerator is capable of running multiple kernels in 
parallel or by employing multiple accelerator units. With a single accelerator, one 
option is to perform the redundant execution in series. However, as this will impact 
the performance, a practical option is to abandon the redundant execution and take 
the system design approach of accepting the service-based parallel processing at 
ASIL-B, and another ASIL-B or higher sub-system for monitoring the result of 
accelerator-model service. 
 4.3 Safety considerations 
Safety is a system design topic. One of the typical issues is that the parallel 
processing hardware technology does not satisfy ASIL-D but only up to ASIL-B. The 
software can be developed based on ASIL-D practices but as the hardware is only 
capable of ASIL-B, as a whole it cannot achieve ASIL-D. The required ASIL for a 
“subsystem” depends on the system functionality it provides – e.g. parallel 
processing subsystem is used for ASIL-B system functionality, which computation 
result is safety-checked by ASIL-D subsystem. Or, one can go duplicate ASIL-B 
subsystem to achieve ASIL-D (though it may be expensive). The design guidelines 
for system design to achieve overall functional safety requirements are out of the 
scope of this document. 
 
To achieve the determinism that is essential for achieving ASIL-D by the service-
based parallel processing, it is advised to follow the approach as discussed in “4.2 
Deterministic execution”.

This synchronization is a pre-condition that guarantees the AT_227_Ipdu is sent out on the next associated slot execution and continues to be transmitted periodically thereafter.
